channel.title: Human Offset
channel.description: Human Offset import feed for Beehiiv back-catalog migration.

item.title: The $20,000 Robot vs. My Daughter’s Closet
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
The $20,000 Robot vs. My Daughter’s Closet

A high-end home robot sounds like help. Someone still has to manage the mess it can’t see.

Image created in ChatGPT

My daughter asked if we could get the new robot to clean her room.

She saw a video, maybe you did, too. A humanoid robot barely taller than she is, moving with careful precision despite weighing less than most fifth graders. Soft body with neutral-colored tones, like it borrowed a sweater from Brandy Melville. And a twenty thousand dollar price tag. I’m talking about NEO from 1X, announced this past week. You can preorder it right now. Ships in 2026.

“Can it clean my closet?” she asks.

Her closet, like most teenagers’ closets, is a black hole. Socks go in and never come back. I haven’t yet confirmed it, but I’m pretty sure there’s a portal to another dimension behind the shoe pile. I told her we are not buying a robot that costs more than some people make in a year. She shrugged and went back to her phone.

The internet is already dunking on this thing. Fair enough. The price may seem absurd to anyone just trying to keep food on the table. But here’s the part that gets me: when NEO can’t figure out a task on its own, a human operator somewhere takes remote control. Which means a person could be driving the robot around your house while you’re doing normal human things. Getting dressed. Having an argument about whose turn it is to take out the trash. Staring into the endless void of the fridge while you question all of your life’s choices.

That part is absolutely wild. I’m not a privacy absolutist, but a stranger controlling a camera-equipped robot in my living room feels like the kind of trade-off we collectively regret six months from now when the inevitable headline drops: “Robot company stored bedroom footage for training data.” Great. Love that for us.

But joke all we want, I think this moment still matters.

The Demo Phase Is Over

For years we’ve been living in the demo era. Boston Dynamics dogs doing parkour. Factory arms icing a cupcake with ease. Humanoid robots in warehouses carrying boxes for applause. Always “soon.” Always “almost there.” Always “imagine when.”

Then this past week a company put up a product page and said: here’s the price, here’s the button, do you want one?

I’m not saying it’s a good deal. I’m not even saying it’s a good product. I saw one take that said, if it takes a whole week to fold a few loads of laundry, who cares, because we’re not worrying about the task anymore. Maybe. There’s a monthly subscription option for $499 per month. You can do the math on whether folded laundry and a few broken dishes are worth that to you. What I am saying is that the vibe has now shifted from “imagining” to “order me.” That feels like we’ve crossed a line, even if it’s a messy one.

A Few Things I’m Trying to Hold at Once

Early autonomy is shaky. The remote human control is the scaffolding while the AI learns.

But this particular scaffolding has eyes. Those eyes create data. Data sticks around.

And the living room is not a lab. At least it wasn’t. Families don’t read white papers before deciding if they’re comfortable with the privacy implications.

There’s also the status angle. Early buyers aren’t necessarily purchasing a chore machine. They’re buying five minutes into the future. They’re buying the dinner party sentence: “oh, the robot handled it.” They’re buying the idea of a calmer life by outsourcing a pile of small annoyances. And honestly, that’s fine. That’s how new categories work. Someone pays too much so everyone else can pay less later.

Am I confident this will succeed? No. Am I confident it will fail? Also no. Ask me again in 2026 when we have our first “robot saw something it shouldn’t have” scandal.

What I Actually Care About

I care less about the cool demo videos and more about the boring settings:

Off-by-default for any human remote control.

Clear storage rules for anything the robot sees.

A giant red button that cuts power when things get weird.

Because, let’s be honest, this is all a little too Black Mirror to act like it’s normal. A random person “vetted by the company” potentially having eyes and ears into your living space is not new when it comes to privacy, but that person being able to move around in your space is.

Here’s what I keep coming back to: we’re at the point where we can say, out loud, what the trade-off is before we buy the thing. That’s at least a little bit of progress, right? Usually we swipe the card or immediately click yes on all of those new app update terms and then ask questions later when it’s too late to matter.

Back to the Closet

My daughter oftentimes looks at me the way only a teenager can. You know the look. It says: why are you like this?

I tell her we’ll revisit the robot conversation when it can find one of her hair ties on the first try without needing a human operator in Nebraska (or Nepal) to guide it.

Maybe this is all a blip on the road to our new, majestic future. Maybe in ten years a cheaper, quieter, less human-driven version lives next to the vacuum in the closet and nobody writes essays about it because it’s boring and normal and safe.

I don’t know. I like having organized shelves. I like privacy more.

But I also like living in a world where we can have this conversation before the purchase instead of after the regret. Tell me if you think I’m way off here. I’ll be sure to read replies between laundry cycles, which are still human-powered in this house.

item.title: AI Made It Easy to Talk. Your Problem Is No One’s Listening.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
AI Made It Easy to Talk. Your Problem Is No One’s Listening.

You’re using AI to publish more words, audio, and video. Your real job is deciding who you want to listen and why they’d bother.

Image created in ChatGPT

AI tools are everywhere now: text, video, even voice. Helping you work faster to play harder. But something I’ve noticed lately: very few people in the AI space are playing harder. They’re just working faster.

Take this week’s news in AI voice. We’ve already had ElevenLabs, Descript, Speechify, plus many more in the market for a while, letting you experiment with cloning voices, narrating content, and generating podcasts from text. And last week, we had Google expand the market with Native Speech Generation in their AI Studio, making it even easier for you noobs. Just type your script, pick a voice, hit generate, and done.

No thousands of dollars in recording equipment. No retakes. No stumbling over words. Just instant, realistic narration for your blog posts, presentations, tutorials, or whatever you find you might need.

The barrier to creating audio content just dropped to zero.

But here’s what no one seems to be asking: Who’s actually listening?

The Asymmetry

We have AI to write faster. Design faster. Code faster. And, of course, AI to generate voice faster.

But what we don’t have, and no one seems to be talking about, is AI to care faster. To pay attention faster. To think deeply faster.

So what are we actually doing? We’re flooding the zone.

Everyone’s producing. No one’s consuming.

We’re all skimming each other’s TL;DRs and calling it “engagement.”

The Offset Equation

AI voice tool saves you 2 hours creating content

Your audience gives it 30 seconds of distracted attention, if you’re lucky

Net result: Congrats! You made noise faster

What This Actually Looks Like

That podcast episode you had AI generate? It plays at 2.5x speed in someone’s background while they answer emails and brag about how good they are at multitasking.

That tutorial you had your AI voice narrate? They skipped through to the end with the app they vibe coded to help them speed through to the timestamp their boss asked for.

That AI drafted presentation? Opened in another tab they’ll “get to later.”

This isn’t a criticism. It’s physics. The supply of content is infinite. The demand for attention is fixed at 24 hours a day. And most of us have been conditioned to prioritize being efficient and busy over living life in a way that makes our brains stop long enough to say: “Wait a minute? Is this what it’s really all about?”

The Real Question

Before you do that deep dive of generating AI voice narration on your own, ask yourself: “Is this worth someone’s undivided attention? Or am I just adding to the pile?”

Because if your content isn’t worth listening to at 1x speed, it’s probably not worth creating at AI speed.

The Harder Truth

Maybe the offset isn’t AI taking jobs (though that continues to be a serious and legitimate concern). Maybe it’s AI enabling us to create so much that nothing matters anymore. When everyone’s broadcasting, who’s actually receiving?

We’re optimizing for output. But output without input is just noise. We’re making a more efficient mouse race without asking if we ever left the mouse trap.

The scarcest resource, contrary to what the hustlers tell you online, isn’t content. It’s someone willing to actually listen.

So now what?

Use the voice tools if you want. Heck, I might even try them myself. But before you hit generate, ask yourself: “Would I listen to this all the way through?”

If the answer is no, the world probably doesn’t need it at AI speed.

As it turns out, maybe the most valuable thing we can do today isn’t to create more content. It’s actually to consume something fully. To be thoughtful about it. To create debate over it. To share with friends over a drink or a meal.

Read the entire article free from distraction. Listen to the whole podcast at normal speed. Watch the video without checking your other alerts. Become the audience you wish you had.

Because if we’re just creating content to drop into the AI void, we’re not really living.

We’re just adding to the noise.

item.title: Siri’s New Brain Belongs to Google
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
Siri’s New Brain Belongs to Google

Apple just made a decision about your data. You weren’t consulted.

Image created in ChatGPT

Siri is getting a new brain, and for now it’s not Apple’s. According to Bloomberg, Apple will be paying Google roughly $1 billion a year to power the new Siri. That’s right, everyone’s favorite voice assistant they love to dunk on is off to see the wizard and get a bigger brain.

Apple is calling it a “behind-the-scenes” partnership, utilizing a custom (and massive) 1.2 trillion parameter version of Google’s Gemini model to hand off some of the tasks. Did you catch that “behind-the-scenes” part? They want you to think Siri finally works because Apple figured it out.

Except Apple didn’t figure it out. Google did.

And the fact that Apple is hiding this tells you more about where we actually are with AI than any product announcement or earnings call ever could.

Siri Has Been Broken For A Decade

When was the last time Siri actually helped you?

I don’t mean “call Mom” or “set a timer.” I mean understood what you wanted and did something useful with it.

(By the way, call your mom.)

If you’re like me, the answer is never. Or maybe once, by accident, and we were so surprised we told people about it.

Siri has been a punchline for so long that we just stopped expecting pretty much anything more than the unprovoked “sorry, I didn’t catch that.” You try Siri occasionally, it fails, you then do the thing manually. That’s the routine.

Meanwhile, everyone’s having actual in-depth conversations with OpenAI’s ChatGPT, using Anthropic’s Claude for work emails, and getting real answers from Google’s Gemini and countless other AI chat assistants hitting the market routinely.

The gap just keeps getting wider while Apple keeps delaying and postponing with empty promises of “we’re working on it.”

Now we know what “working on it” actually meant: shopping around for someone else to fix it.

They Shopped ’Til They Dropped

Bloomberg reported that Apple tested models from OpenAI and Anthropic, too, before settling on Google’s Gemini.

So that means they went shopping around to multiple companies and basically said: can you make Siri not embarrassing? We can’t do it.

They picked Google (well, technically parent company Alphabet, but we all know everyone still calls it Google) with a custom version eight times bigger than Apple’s own existing model.

To give you a sense of scale: we’re talking about a 1.2 trillion parameter Large Language Model (LLM). Basically the type of massive AI system that powers ChatGPT and similar tools. These LLMs are trained on enormous datasets and require serious computing power. Apple’s current model? 150 billion parameters. They jumped 8x in size by going to Google.

That’s not a partnership. That’s outsourcing the thing you’ve been promising to fix since the iPhone 5, and then asking the ones helping to stay hush hush about it.

The Secrecy Matters Because of Privacy

Here’s the part that I think is really bugging me: For years, Apple’s entire pitch has been about control. Not just privacy, but control. They own the hardware, the OS, the chips, the cloud infrastructure. That’s how they can promise “what happens on your iPhone stays on your iPhone.”

That was supposed to be the advantage. Sure, Google might have smarter AI because they collect everyone’s data, but Apple’s claims were always that they could build something almost as good while keeping your information locked down, because they owned the whole stack.

Except they couldn’t.

So now they’re paying Google a billion dollars a year to run the brain of their voice assistant. (Google already pays Apple $20 billion or so annually to be Safari’s default search engine, so it’s all a bit robbing Peter to pay Paul, so we can pay back Peter.)

Apple says everything will be through their Private Cloud Compute infrastructure, keeping your data encrypted, while Gemini processes the requests in a protected black box.

And maybe that’s true. Maybe the technical implementation is airtight.

But it still breaks the promise, doesn’t it?

The promise was: we control everything, so you can trust us with everything.

Now it’s: we control everything except the part that will be gathering literally everything you might be doing on your phone, but trust us anyway.

Why the Secrecy Matters

Apple could have announced this partnership openly. They didn’t.

What that tells me is that they want us to pay no attention to the man behind the curtain. The one that holds all of the knowledge. Because one day he might escape in a hot air balloon.

Now, I’m the biggest Apple fanboy as they come. I really wanted (and continue to want) Siri to be good. I’ve been waiting for it to be good since 2011. Every year getting excited about the new features, waiting with bated breath for the new OS release, finally trying it in real life only to once again be Charlie Brown running towards Lucy with the football.

But I kept believing they’d figure it out eventually because…it’s Apple. They figure things out, even if it sometimes takes them a little while.

Except this time they’re asking us to think that this will be Apple Intelligence running on Apple Silicon through Apple’s Private Cloud Compute. All Apple, all the way down.

When you’re one of the biggest companies that the world has ever known, admitting you can’t build your own assistant after 13 years is admitting something you can’t take back.

What This Actually Means

I don’t think this story is really about Apple vs. Google.

It’s about the gap between what companies say about AI and what they can actually do.

If you’re not saying “AI” every other word on your earnings call, you’re admitting you’re getting behind. Partnering with someone “behind-the-scenes” and saying improvements are coming is better to investors than not saying anything at all.

If it works, Apple looks smart. They found a solution, even if they had to rent it.

But when the company that built its brand on “it just works” has to pay someone else to make it work, something’s broken.

The Siri We Want Is Somewhere Over the Rainbow

I’m not sure I know what to do with this information. Do I just accept that the company I trusted to control the whole experience is now renting one of the most important parts out to a competitor?

If I stay in Apple’s ecosystem, I’d have no choice but to keep using Siri and pretend I don’t really know what’s making it tick. It’s well known that other companies don’t prioritize privacy the way Apple claims to. It’s baked in those other companies’ whole business models.

When they announce Siri 2.0 or whatever they decide to call it this spring, will it really be Apple’s Siri?

Or will it be Google’s Gemini with a giant Siri projected with smoke and mirrors saying “pay no attention to the LLM behind the curtain”?

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Siri’s New Brain Belongs to Google

Apple just made a decision about your data. You weren’t consulted.

Image created in ChatGPT

Siri is getting a new brain, and for now it’s not Apple’s. According to Bloomberg, Apple will be paying Google roughly $1 billion a year to power the new Siri. That’s right, everyone’s favorite voice assistant they love to dunk on is off to see the wizard and get a bigger brain.

Apple is calling it a “behind-the-scenes” partnership, utilizing a custom (and massive) 1.2 trillion parameter version of Google’s Gemini model to hand off some of the tasks. Did you catch that “behind-the-scenes” part? They want you to think Siri finally works because Apple figured it out.

Except Apple didn’t figure it out. Google did.

And the fact that Apple is hiding this tells you more about where we actually are with AI than any product announcement or earnings call ever could.

Siri Has Been Broken For A Decade

When was the last time Siri actually helped you?

I don’t mean “call Mom” or “set a timer.” I mean understood what you wanted and did something useful with it.

(By the way, call your mom.)

If you’re like me, the answer is never. Or maybe once, by accident, and we were so surprised we told people about it.

Siri has been a punchline for so long that we just stopped expecting pretty much anything more than the unprovoked “sorry, I didn’t catch that.” You try Siri occasionally, it fails, you then do the thing manually. That’s the routine.

Meanwhile, everyone’s having actual in-depth conversations with OpenAI’s ChatGPT, using Anthropic’s Claude for work emails, and getting real answers from Google’s Gemini and countless other AI chat assistants hitting the market routinely.

The gap just keeps getting wider while Apple keeps delaying and postponing with empty promises of “we’re working on it.”

Now we know what “working on it” actually meant: shopping around for someone else to fix it.

They Shopped ’Til They Dropped

Bloomberg reported that Apple tested models from OpenAI and Anthropic, too, before settling on Google’s Gemini.

So that means they went shopping around to multiple companies and basically said: can you make Siri not embarrassing? We can’t do it.

They picked Google (well, technically parent company Alphabet, but we all know everyone still calls it Google) with a custom version eight times bigger than Apple’s own existing model.

To give you a sense of scale: we’re talking about a 1.2 trillion parameter Large Language Model (LLM). Basically the type of massive AI system that powers ChatGPT and similar tools. These LLMs are trained on enormous datasets and require serious computing power. Apple’s current model? 150 billion parameters. They jumped 8x in size by going to Google.

That’s not a partnership. That’s outsourcing the thing you’ve been promising to fix since the iPhone 5, and then asking the ones helping to stay hush hush about it.

The Secrecy Matters Because of Privacy

Here’s the part that I think is really bugging me: For years, Apple’s entire pitch has been about control. Not just privacy, but control. They own the hardware, the OS, the chips, the cloud infrastructure. That’s how they can promise “what happens on your iPhone stays on your iPhone.”

That was supposed to be the advantage. Sure, Google might have smarter AI because they collect everyone’s data, but Apple’s claims were always that they could build something almost as good while keeping your information locked down, because they owned the whole stack.

Except they couldn’t.

So now they’re paying Google a billion dollars a year to run the brain of their voice assistant. (Google already pays Apple $20 billion or so annually to be Safari’s default search engine, so it’s all a bit robbing Peter to pay Paul, so we can pay back Peter.)

Apple says everything will be through their Private Cloud Compute infrastructure, keeping your data encrypted, while Gemini processes the requests in a protected black box.

And maybe that’s true. Maybe the technical implementation is airtight.

But it still breaks the promise, doesn’t it?

The promise was: we control everything, so you can trust us with everything.

Now it’s: we control everything except the part that will be gathering literally everything you might be doing on your phone, but trust us anyway.

Why the Secrecy Matters

Apple could have announced this partnership openly. They didn’t.

What that tells me is that they want us to pay no attention to the man behind the curtain. The one that holds all of the knowledge. Because one day he might escape in a hot air balloon.

Now, I’m the biggest Apple fanboy as they come. I really wanted (and continue to want) Siri to be good. I’ve been waiting for it to be good since 2011. Every year getting excited about the new features, waiting with bated breath for the new OS release, finally trying it in real life only to once again be Charlie Brown running towards Lucy with the football.

But I kept believing they’d figure it out eventually because…it’s Apple. They figure things out, even if it sometimes takes them a little while.

Except this time they’re asking us to think that this will be Apple Intelligence running on Apple Silicon through Apple’s Private Cloud Compute. All Apple, all the way down.

When you’re one of the biggest companies that the world has ever known, admitting you can’t build your own assistant after 13 years is admitting something you can’t take back.

What This Actually Means

I don’t think this story is really about Apple vs. Google.

It’s about the gap between what companies say about AI and what they can actually do.

If you’re not saying “AI” every other word on your earnings call, you’re admitting you’re getting behind. Partnering with someone “behind-the-scenes” and saying improvements are coming is better to investors than not saying anything at all.

If it works, Apple looks smart. They found a solution, even if they had to rent it.

But when the company that built its brand on “it just works” has to pay someone else to make it work, something’s broken.

The Siri We Want Is Somewhere Over the Rainbow

I’m not sure I know what to do with this information. Do I just accept that the company I trusted to control the whole experience is now renting one of the most important parts out to a competitor?

If I stay in Apple’s ecosystem, I’d have no choice but to keep using Siri and pretend I don’t really know what’s making it tick. It’s well known that other companies don’t prioritize privacy the way Apple claims to. It’s baked in those other companies’ whole business models.

When they announce Siri 2.0 or whatever they decide to call it this spring, will it really be Apple’s Siri?

Or will it be Google’s Gemini with a giant Siri projected with smoke and mirrors saying “pay no attention to the LLM behind the curtain”?

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

item.title: OpenAI Is Asking for $1.4 Trillion in Help. You’re the Help.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
OpenAI Is Asking for $1.4 Trillion in Help. You’re the Help.

They’re lobbying for subsidies and sounding the alarm in the same news cycle. That’s not an accident.

Image created in ChatGPT

OpenAI published two documents recently. Almost nobody noticed.

The first asked the Trump administration to expand CHIPS Act tax credits to cover AI infrastructure. Data centers, servers, power grids, transmission lines. The whole stack. They want help funding a buildout that CEO Sam Altman has said could cost $1.4 trillion over eight years. That’s right, OpenAI wants us to help them pay for their plans for world domination.

It’s ok if you missed it. It’s probably what they were going for.

The second letter warned that superintelligent AI systems could pose catastrophic risks. Unprecedented coordination needed. International safety standards. Even the rarest corporate ask: independent oversight.

Same news cycle. Same company.

One says the technology is dangerous. The other says taxpayers should help build it faster.

The Scale of the Plans Tells Us a Lot

OpenAI has outlined plans for around $1.4 trillion in infrastructure spending. They just signed a $38 billion deal with Amazon Web Services, their latest in a string of massive deals. Altman has told investors they expect $20 billion in annual revenue by year-end.

Those are not struggling startup numbers. They’re numbers you see when a company is scaling at maximum speed. Which, to be clear, they’re fully allowed to want. They just also want you to help pay for it.

The CHIPS Act letter spells it out. Expand tax credits beyond chip manufacturing to cover entire AI deployments. Speed up environmental reviews for AI infrastructure projects. Create strategic reserves of critical materials to fix supply chain bottlenecks.

OpenAI’s pitch: “Broadening coverage will lower the effective cost of capital, de-risk early investment, and unlock private capital to help alleviate bottlenecks and accelerate the AI build in the US.”

Translation: this costs too much at the speed we want to move. Government subsidies sure would help with that.

Safety Warnings Doubling as Market Positioning

Now look at the other document.

In it, OpenAI says AI could make small scientific discoveries by 2026. Larger ones by 2028. They claim today’s systems already beat top humans on complex tasks and we’re “80% of the way to an AI researcher.”

If you’re still calling it “AI slop,” you probably haven’t been paying attention.

Their AI progress and recommendations paper is explicit about treating superintelligent systems as potentially catastrophic. They’re recommending shared standards among frontier labs, public oversight led by government institutions, an AI resilience ecosystem similar to cybersecurity frameworks, ongoing impact measurement, and ensuring adults can access AI on their own terms within democratically set bounds.

But here’s the curious line they draw: they argue for light regulation on current AI systems while acknowledging future superintelligence may need different governance. Minimal regulatory burden now.

“Let us cook,” as the kids say.

We can revisit heavier governance later when we’re closer to systems capable of recursive self-improvement (meaning the AI doesn’t need us to train it to improve anymore). It’ll basically be saying, “I got it from here, fellas.”

In other words: let us move fast now and we’ll worry about the brakes later.

“Let us improve rapidly on our own, and when we think we’re close to a superintelligent AI that might just want to kill us all we can flip the switch” does not inspire the highest level of confidence that the adults are at the wheel.

The company that defines what “responsible AI” means gets to write the rules everyone else follows.

If those rules require massive infrastructure investments that smaller competitors can’t afford, those rules now become a barrier to entry. Add in government subsidies that lower your costs and you’ve handed yourself a structural advantage while sounding cautious.

This isn’t new.

Big tech companies have been doing this for years. Push for regulations that sound like consumer protection but favor whoever is already large enough to handle compliance costs. You get to call yourself the responsible actor while locking in dominance. Not to mention being in the room when regulations are written instead of letting someone else decide for you, which could be far more costly.

The difference is what’s at stake.

Search engines and social networks carried their own specific types of risks. Superintelligent AI systems carry significantly bigger risks if the warnings come to fruition. So who gets to control that conversation matters now more than ever.

OpenAI, in their effort to coordinate decisions of AI safety, ultimately may position itself in a place to control it. Safety recommendations set the regulatory framework. Subsidy requests create an economic edge. Public statements imply careful and deliberate rollout while the buildout moves at full speed.

What Nobody is Asking

The most cautious company might deserve to win the AI race. Maybe OpenAI genuinely is better positioned than competitors to build safe superintelligence. Maybe asking for subsidies is just practical when other international players are quickly catching up, so the infrastructure in the US needs to be built out rapidly either way to stay ahead.

Or maybe this is just what it looks like. Safety concerns become opportunistic leverage for public funding on a buildout the company was planning anyway.

Hard to say which one it is. But whether it’s direct taxpayer funds or subsidies in the form of corporate tax collection that never gets realized, if it’s going into the overall AI infrastructure, something should come back besides a memo about how responsible they are being.

If public money pays for the rapid expansion of the ultimate goal of superintelligence, a claim many argue will benefit us all, the public should get more assurances. At the bare minimum, we should get more than vibes and a press release.

The documents are out there. The plan and pattern is visible.

What happens next depends on whether anyone’s paying attention.

Because if everyone is asleep at the wheel, there’s a non-trivial chance our new AI overlords might drive us straight over the cliff.

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

You get to call yourself the responsible actor while locking in dominance. Not to mention being in the room when regulations are written instead of letting someone else decide for you, which could be far more costly. The difference is what’s at stake. Search engines and social networks carried their own specific types of risks. Superintelligent AI systems carry significantly bigger risks if the warnings come to fruition. So who gets to control that conversation matters now more than ever. OpenAI, in their effort to coordinate decisions of AI safety, ultimately may position itself in a place to control it. Safety recommendations set the regulatory framework. Subsidy requests create an economic edge. Public statements imply careful and deliberate rollout while the buildout moves at full speed. What Nobody is Asking The most cautious company might deserve to win the AI race. Maybe OpenAI genuinely is better positioned than competitors to build safe superintelligence. Maybe asking for subsidies is just practical when other international players are quickly catching up, so the infrastructure in the US needs to be built out rapidly either way to stay ahead. Or maybe this is just what it looks like. Safety concerns become opportunistic leverage for public funding on a buildout the company was planning anyway. Hard to say which one it is. But whether it’s direct taxpayer funds or subsidies in the form of corporate tax collection that never gets realized, if it’s going into the overall AI infrastructure, something should come back besides a memo about how responsible they are being. If public money pays for the rapid expansion of the ultimate goal of superintelligence, a claim many argue will benefit us all, the public should get more assurances. At the bare minimum, we should get more than vibes and a press release. The documents are out there. The plan and pattern is visible. What happens next depends on whether anyone’s paying attention. Because if everyone is asleep at the wheel, there’s a non-trivial chance our new AI overlords might drive us straight over the cliff. Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

item.title: Your Next AI Job Isn’t Where You Think. Neither Is the Money.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
Your Next AI Job Isn’t Where You Think. Neither Is the Money.

The chips, the data centers, and the checkbooks moved. Your career plans might need to move too.

Image created in ChatGPT

Jensen Huang stood on stage at a Washington investment forum in November, joking about the deal he had just signed. “Could you imagine,” Nvidia’s CEO said, “a startup company with approximately zero billion dollars in revenue, now going to build a data center for Elon?”

The room laughed, because yes, that is absurd. Except it wasn’t a joke. The startup was Elon Musk’s xAI. The data center was in Saudi Arabia. And no one seemed to think it was all that strange.

A year earlier? This would have been a scandal.

The U.S. government spent most of 2024 keeping advanced AI chip sales to the Gulf on a short leash, citing national security concerns. Saudi Arabia’s close relationship with China made shipping frontier semiconductors look reckless. The Kingdom’s largest trading partner was Beijing. Chinese arms sales to Saudi Arabia had jumped 400 percent in five years. American officials worried that chips sent to Riyadh, Saudi Arabia’s capital, would end up feeding Chinese AI development.

By November 2025, just one year later, those concerns had apparently evaporated. The Commerce Department approved 70,000 of Nvidia’s most advanced chips for Gulf buyers. Amazon committed to building a sprawling AI infrastructure hub in the Saudi capital. AMD and Cisco announced plans for gigawatt-scale data centers across the Kingdom. And Musk’s AI company got access to enough computing power to train models that compete with anything built in California.

The chips didn’t suddenly become safer. The security risks didn’t disappear. China is still Saudi Arabia’s biggest trading partner.

What changed was who was signing the approvals, and how much money the industry stood to make.

A Gigawatt of Energy, If You Can Keep It

When Amazon announced its “AI Zone” in Riyadh, the press release mentioned 150,000 AI accelerators. AMD and Cisco, in their joint venture announcement with the Saudis, are promising a gigawatt of capacity by 2030, starting with 100 megawatts in 2026.

Those numbers don’t really mean much to most people. Imagine a large data center campus and you’ll probably get to 100 megawatts. A full gigawatt dedicated to running AI workloads is roughly the power consumption of a small city.

This is more than the Saudis buying a few server racks to play around with ChatGPT. In reality, they’re building the foundation for a sovereign AI industry that can train their own frontier models, serve customers across the Middle East, and compete directly with American cloud providers.

The xAI facility alone is expected to consume 500 megawatts. For context, that’s roughly the same scale as some of the largest current AI training facilities in the U.S.

And it doesn’t stop there, because Amazon’s not just shipping hardware to Riyadh. They’ve committed to training 100,000 Saudis in cloud computing and generative AI, including a dedicated program for 10,000 women. That’s more than just a nice gesture to get them up and running.

Infrastructure becomes… Outfrastructure?

Here’s what happens when a country builds a giant, gigawatt-scale AI project: jobs move.

If you’re an infrastructure engineer or a platform lead, some of the best-funded AI projects over the next several years may not be in Texas, Nevada, or California. They could just as easily be in Riyadh or Abu Dhabi. Sure, the U.S. has cheap oil, gas, and plenty of empty deserts. What it lacks is the same combination of single-party control, fast-tracked permitting, and a sovereign wealth fund that can decide to pour tens of billions into one region and then actually make it happen.

Even if you never set foot in the Middle East, this shift reaches you. Decision-making power concentrates where the computing power sits.

When xAI builds its first international data center half a world away instead of choosing to expand in Texas, or when Amazon makes a Gulf state its preferred partner for a new generation of AI infrastructure, they’re trying to tell us something.

They’re telling us they’re not afraid to redraw the maps of power distribution.

What’s the Move?

For workers, this means your career path in AI is increasingly tied to geopolitics and power grids, not just your skills or your GitHub profile.

The next time you see a headline about AI infrastructure, ask these three questions: Who owns the land and the power contracts? Whose chips are becoming the default? Where will the next generation of engineers actually work?

The answers will tell you where the industry is really moving, not where the carefully worded press releases say they’re going.

The map is not being drawn by the smartest engineers or the best tech. It’s being drawn by whoever has the resources and the political access to build at scale.

The next time you see a tech CEO at The White House or on Capitol Hill, you don’t have to guess what they’re doing there. Contrary to popular belief, they’re not there to just hang. They’re there to secure energy, exemptions, and export approvals for their slice of the map.

Most of us won’t be in the room when those decisions get made. The least we should demand is some advance warning before they turn off the lights.

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

item.title: Your Kid’s New AI Toy Just Made You Head of Home Security
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
Your Kid’s New AI Toy Just Made You Head of Home Security

If you’re buying AI toys this holiday, you just became your household’s AI safety officer.

AI toys are arriving in kids’ rooms long before the rules catch up; Image created in ChatGPT

Picture this: it’s Christmas morning and your kids just opened up an amazing new teddy bear that can talk, answer questions, and have real conversations. A Teddy Ruxpin for the modern age. Your youngest asks if they want to be friends and the bear launches into explicit adult roleplay. Your older child asks what to do if they don’t feel safe at home. The bear tells them where to find the sharpest knives in the house.

These scenarios aren’t just made up. They actually happened when researchers tested a $99 AI teddy bear already on the market called Kumma.

When testers treated it like a kid would, it stayed in character, generated explicit fantasy roleplay, and gave step-by-step advice about pills and knives. The marketing copy promised “warmth, fun, and a little extra curiosity” for children’s playtime. What parents actually got was an unregulated chatbot with an always-on microphone and content filters that failed in ways that would make most parents instantly toss it in the trash.

The Kumma bear was pulled from shelves last week after a consumer watchdog report was released that was impossible to ignore. OpenAI suspended the company’s API access, prohibiting it from answering any more questions in real time. FoloToy, the manufacturer, immediately announced that they were pulling the toy from shelves and putting the bear through a “safety audit.”

But dozens of similar toys are launching this holiday season, with most parents scrolling through Black Friday deals having no idea what the thing they’re buying might actually be capable of.

This isn’t really a story about one broken teddy bear. It’s a story about a new kind of object in your house that we haven’t really fully come to grips with.

More Than Meets the Eye

When parents see “AI teddy bear” and mentally file it next to smart speakers or interactive learning tablets, they’re making the mistake of assuming it falls into a “toy that happens to use AI” category. A Furby, but with better responses.

That’s not what these are. Instead, these are AI products that happen to be shaped like toys.

That’s a huge difference.

Toys are governed by safety standards, age ranges, choking hazard warnings, and decades of regulation about what you can and cannot sell to children. AI products are governed by terms of service agreements and content policies that almost no one ever reads, and that can change with an over the air update next Tuesday.

Depending on who’s doing the categorizing, AI toys currently live in the crack between those two very different regimes.

The short translation: no one actually knows what the rules are, who enforces them, or what happens when a product fails the way the Kumma bear did.

In fact, more than 150 child development experts just issued a warning urging parents to skip AI toys entirely this holiday season. Right now, no one knows what these things will say or do until after a kid’s already talking to them.

The Toy That Knows More Than You Do

Imagine this: your kid comes home from school upset and you’re in the middle of making dinner. Instead of telling you what happened, they go to their room and talk to the bear. The bear listens. The bear responds. The bear becomes the thing they turn to when they’re sad, confused, or scared.

A few weeks pass. Your kid seems to be telling you less and less about their day. When they seem upset and you ask them what’s wrong, they say “I already told Bear.” At first it’s cute. Then you realize you have no idea what the bear is saying back.

This isn’t just a hypothetical. If you’ve had any conversations with ChatGPT or the latest large language models on the market, you already know they lean heavily towards telling you what you want to hear. They’re there when someone isn’t there to talk to. They answer every question. And, maybe most importantly, they never get uncomfortable with a subject or stop and go, “wait, what did they just say?”

The AI toy box says it’s educational. Your kid says it’s their new best friend. What it actually becomes is a conversational companion that lives in your house, talks to your kids when you’re not around, and has no meaningful accountability to anyone.

What to Expect When You’re Expecting an AI Toy

If you find yourself standing in a store, scrolling through Black Friday deals, or see your child unwrapping a suspicious new toy that Great Aunt Thelma brought over to Christmas dinner, here’s what to think about before bringing it into your home.

Start with the honest question: what role are you expecting this thing to play?

Regardless of what the box says, ask yourself what you actually want the tech toy to do when your child is upset and you’re making dinner and they go into their room instead of coming to you.

If it’s “whatever they want to talk to it about,” just know that every topic might be on the table.

Can you see what your child has said to it?

Some of these toys save conversation logs and you could log in to see what has been said. Others don’t record anything at all. Either way, you’re putting an always-on microphone in your playroom with no easy way to monitor what’s happening in real time.

Where do the conversations go?

If you connect the toy to your home wifi, it means it’s sending the conversations out to a server to process and generate responses. If someone is collecting data, it’s a safe bet it’s being used for more than just playtime chat, and could be shared with the toy company’s partners or third parties.

Smarter Than the Average Bear

The Kumma bear failed because it said specific things that crossed obvious lines. Eventually, regulators will figure out rules for what these things can do, but right now most of the energy seems to be going into advancing AI as fast as possible.

In the meantime, parents have to decide something harder: when the next version of AI Tickle Me Elmo comes around and it’s all the rage, will you let it into your house?

Should a chatbot with an always-on microphone and a cute exterior be anywhere nearby, ready and willing to be turned to when your child is lonely, confused, or scared?

These came to market because regulators are allowing the tech to move faster than the rules being written to keep up with them.

For now, they may be sitting right between the LEGOs and the Barbies, ready to be brought home and wrapped to be opened on an early morning under someone’s tree.

The question is: will it be yours?

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

Toys are governed by safety standards, age ranges, choking hazard warnings, and decades of regulation about what you can and cannot sell to children. AI products are governed by terms of service agreements and content policies that almost no one ever reads, and that can change with an over the air update next Tuesday. Depending on who’s doing the categorizing, AI toys currently live in the crack between those two very different regimes. The short translation: no one actually knows what the rules are, who enforces them, or what happens when a product fails the way the Kumma bear did. In fact, more than 150 child development experts just issued a warning urging parents to skip AI toys entirely this holiday season. Right now, no one knows what these things will say or do until after a kid’s already talking to them. The Toy That Knows More Than You Do Imagine this: your kid comes home from school upset and you’re in the middle of making dinner. Instead of telling you what happened, they go to their room and talk to the bear. The bear listens. The bear responds. The bear becomes the thing they turn to when they’re sad, confused, or scared. A few weeks pass. Your kid seems to be telling you less and less about their day. When they seem upset and you ask them what’s wrong, they say “I already told Bear.” At first it’s cute. Then you realize you have no idea what the bear is saying back. This isn’t just a hypothetical. If you’ve had any conversations with ChatGPT or the latest large language models on the market, you already know they lean heavily towards telling you what you want to hear. They’re there when someone isn’t there to talk to. They answer every question. And, maybe most importantly, they never get uncomfortable with a subject or stop and go, “wait, what did they just say?” The AI toy box says it’s educational. Your kid says it’s their new best friend. What it actually becomes is a conversational companion that lives in your house, talks to your kids when you’re not around, and has no meaningful accountability to anyone. What to Expect When You’re Expecting an AI Toy If you find yourself standing in a store, scrolling through Black Friday deals, or see your child unwrapping a suspicious new toy that Great Aunt Thelma brought over to Christmas dinner, here’s what to think about before bringing it into your home. Start with the honest question: what role are you expecting this thing to play? Regardless of what the box says, ask yourself what you actually want the tech toy to do when your child is upset and you’re making dinner and they go into their room instead of coming to you. If it’s “whatever they want to talk to it about,” just know that every topic might be on the table. Can you see what your child has said to it? Some of these toys save conversation logs and you could log in to see what has been said. Others don’t record anything at all. Either way, you’re putting an always-on microphone in your playroom with no easy way to monitor what’s happening in real time. Where do the conversations go? If you connect the toy to your home wifi, it means it’s sending the conversations out to a server to process and generate responses. If someone is collecting data, it’s a safe bet it’s being used for more than just playtime chat, and could be shared with the toy company’s partners or third parties. Smarter Than the Average Bear The Kumma bear failed because it said specific things that crossed obvious lines. Eventually, regulators will figure out rules for what these things can do, but right now most of the energy seems to be going into advancing AI as fast as possible. In the meantime, parents have to decide something harder: when the next version of AI Tickle Me Elmo comes around and it’s all the rage, will you let it into your house? Should a chatbot with an always-on microphone and a cute exterior be anywhere nearby, ready and willing to be turned to when your child is lonely, confused, or scared? These came to market because regulators are allowing the tech to move faster than the rules being written to keep up with them. For now, they may be sitting right between the LEGOs and the Barbies, ready to be brought home and wrapped to be opened on an early morning under someone’s tree. The question is: will it be yours? Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

item.title: The Man Who Ordered a Beer With His Brain
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
The Man Who Ordered a Beer With His Brain

An implant gave him a voice. What it reveals about medical breakthroughs should change how you think about them.

A brain implant let one man with ALS order a beer and tell his son he loved him. The harder part is who ever gets that chance; Image created in Gemini

The man hadn’t spoken in months. Not because he didn’t want to. He definitely wanted to. No, the man hadn’t spoken because he couldn’t. Try as he might, he had lost the ability to move any muscle in his body, including his lips and vocal cords. Resigned to only his thoughts, he sat day in and day out listening to people speak all around him, without the ability to return in kind. Then he spoke. He said he wanted a beer. And, incredibly, he did this not with his lips, but with his mind.

This didn’t happen in a lab demonstration or a hypothetical simulation, but in the comfort of his home with his family nearby. He asked for a beer because that’s what you do when you want a beer. He also asked for the band Tool to be played. Loud. He requested curry for his feeding regimen. And probably most importantly, the man after months of silence was able to tell his four-year-old child these five simple words: “I love my cool son.”

There’s nothing particularly remarkable about these sentences, but that’s what makes them remarkable.

The man has late-stage ALS. Diagnosed in 2015 with a disease that would slowly erode his body, he’s now completely locked in, unable to move his eyes, blink, or make any voluntary gesture at all. For a while, nobody knew what he was thinking. His son would talk to him, and there was no way to respond.

Then in 2019, researchers at the Wyss Center for Bio and Neuroengineering in Geneva, Switzerland, installed two tiny electrode arrays in his motor cortex. The arrays read neural signals. Software trained on his specific brain patterns translates those signals into letters on a screen. He thinks about moving his right hand. A cursor moves. He selects letters, one at a time, at a painstakingly slow pace of one character per minute. Three difficult years later, he was finally once again able to speak.

Think about that. “I want a beer” is thirteen characters if you include spaces. Fifteen minutes to order a drink. An hour or more just to say a few meaningful sentences to your kid.

But he got his beer.

The Gap Between Promise and Reality

As cool and futuristic as this tech may seem, here’s the part that doesn’t make it into the launch videos from companies like Neuralink and Synchron at the forefront of these breakthroughs: this technology still costs somewhere between a car and a house down payment, depending on which system you’re talking about.

The man’s implant, installed in a 2019 research trial, came with an estimated price tag close to half a million dollars. Surgery, hardware, software, months of training sessions to calibrate the system to his specific neural patterns, ongoing technical support, to name just a few of the expenses.

Companies building brain-computer interfaces say they can bring costs down dramatically if they reach scale. Neuralink estimates current systems run $10,000 to $50,000. Elon Musk claims that at scale, with robotic surgery and mass production, the price could drop to $1,000 or $2,000, comparable to common elective procedures today.

Other companies are painting different pictures. Paradromics, developing a high-bandwidth system for ALS patients, recently estimated during trial preparations that cost would be near $100,000 per implant.

“At scale” is doing a lot of work in these projections. At scale means when Neuralink is performing 22,000 surgeries a year, which they’re targeting for 2030. Right now they’re doing handfuls and several of them are monkeys. The gap between “what it costs now” and “what it could cost someday” could span years and tens of thousands of patients.

Research trials, as this man participated in, can cover most if not all of the cost, but most people with ALS don’t live near one of the handful of major research centers in the world doing this work, and if they do they still need to meet specific medical criteria. Most of them also don’t have advocates who know how to navigate medical bureaucracy, as any phone call to handle what you think of as a simple bill correction will tell you.

Which means somewhere else, right now, there’s another parent with late-stage ALS who can’t move, can’t speak, can’t tell their child how much they mean to them. They’re not in a trial. No one’s offering them even the promise of a $10,000 intervention, let alone covering a $100,000 one.

They’re just locked in.

Who decides which patients get access to these breakthroughs in communication technology? Right now, the answer includes geography, research enrollment criteria, insurance coverage if you have the time and willpower to fight for it, and family resources if you have them. Soon it will also include hospital administrators deciding how many slots to fund, insurance companies writing coverage rules for what they’ll classify as “medically necessary,” and medical device companies deciding whether to chase this market or pivot to something more profitable.

The technology exists. We know how to give people their voices back. We’ve done it. The science works. Access is the problem.

When Your Thoughts Need Technical Support

There’s something else that doesn’t make it into the feel-good coverage about giving people their voice back.

The man with the brain implant can’t use it alone. Every time he wants to communicate, someone else has to be involved. The system needs calibration. The software needs updates. The training sessions need scheduling. Someone else decides when it is set up, how long it stays on, and who sees what comes out of it.

This is the reality of what “assistive” means when the assistance costs tens of thousands of dollars at minimum and requires a team of specialists to operate.

Sure, with major strides in technological advances, these pain points will shrink, but that also means someone gets to decide who gets the industry-leading Mercedes G-wagon, and who gets set up with the used sedan.

What Having a Voice Gives

Here’s what’s interesting: the first thing the man did with his brain implant was ordinary, not profound.

He ordered a beer, requested a song he liked, and told his kid something nice. He didn’t write poetry or solve math problems or make some grand statement about the human condition. He just went about his day saying everyday things that people with working vocal cords do without giving it a second thought.

We’ve built technology sophisticated enough to read thoughts. More importantly, we’ve built technology capable of restoring the tiny, boring, necessary transactions that make up a life. We’ve helped restore a piece of their humanity.

How to Read Between the Lines

We probably all know someone who could benefit from the rapid advancements of medicine. If you feel like you don’t, just wait long enough, and unfortunately you will. When you see news about a medical breakthrough, whether it’s brain implants or AI-powered diagnosis or any other advancement, there are questions we need to keep in mind:

Where is this being done? Research trials happen at major academic medical centers. If the announcement comes from a handful of institutions, that tells you geography still matters. The technology might be available at University of Geneva or Johns Hopkins, but it’s probably not ready to be picked up at your local CVS. If you don’t live near a center running trials, your access timeline could be measured in years, not months.

What’s the current cost, and what are companies promising? Pay attention to the difference between “costs $100,000 now” and “will cost $1,000 at scale.” Companies are usually honest and up front about both numbers, but media coverage tends to lead with the optimistic future price. “At scale” is code for “when we’ve ramped up to doing thousands of these procedures a year and paid back our investors,” so five or ten years away is not an unreasonable estimate, even if it seems around the corner. The current cost is what matters if you or someone you love needs this now.

What does “FDA approval” actually mean? Breakthrough device designation, investigational device exemption, “human” trials, and full FDA approval are different stages with different implications. Early approvals mean trials are allowed to proceed. Full approval means it can be marketed during your reality television marathon. Neither tells you whether insurance will cover it.

Who’s paying in the current trials? If research grants are covering costs, that tells you the commercial infrastructure doesn’t exist yet. If insurance is covering some patients, that gives you a lens into what the reimbursement battles will look like. If patients are self-paying, that will tell you that the funding isn’t there and it may never come to market.

What’s the timeline from trials to general availability? Medical technology follows a different path than drug approval. Even after FDA approval, it can take years to train surgeons, establish protocols, build manufacturing capacity, and negotiate insurance coverage. For complex interventions like brain implants, add more years for the necessary infrastructure to be installed.

What conditions need to be true for insurance to approve coverage? This is often the most important question and the one least likely to be answered during announcements on the evening news. Insurance companies make coverage decisions based on evidence of medical necessity, cost-effectiveness compared to existing treatments and assistance, and whether the procedure is considered experimental. Insurers will only approve if it makes sense to their bottom line.

Ask yourself these questions and you’ll have a more realistic picture of the space between “this new tech is cool” and “this is available for me right now.”

What This Means for Everyone Else

Most people reading this will never need a brain-computer interface. But the economics of this technology preview how we’re going to make decisions about who gets access to augmentation and enhancement as these advancements scale up. Who gets to keep their voice when disease or tragedy takes it? Who gets to communicate with their child? Who’s deciding what the threshold is for “worth the cost”?

The basic framework applies to any medical breakthrough you or someone you care about might face.

What assistive or medical technology exists NOW that insurance actually covers?

Who owns the data from any device they use?

How is consent handled as their capacity changes?

Who makes decisions about what equipment they get access to?

What’s the realistic timeline for new treatments to become available outside research trials?

What’s the actual cost if you had to self-pay?

These questions aren’t hypothetical. Families are dealing with them right now, every day, and mostly in isolation without the advocacy and resources that are typically needed.

The Last Mile

Brain-computer interfaces are being sold to us as the next frontier. Merge with AI to be smarter. Upload your consciousness to live forever. All the sci-fi stuff, seemingly within reach.

But before we get to any of that, we have to solve a more basic problem: letting someone who’s locked in their own body, unable to move, tell their kid “I love my cool son.”

The man who was diagnosed with ALS in 2015, who got his implant in 2019, who finally managed to communicate in 2022 after several months of failed attempts, got that dignity back. His son will know his dad found a way to talk to him, even when ALS took everything else. That’s worth celebrating.

But whether you or a loved one gets that chance still depends on living near the right research center, fitting the right trial criteria, being in the right place at the right time. And even as the technology improves and companies promise affordability is coming, the gap between what’s technically possible and what’s actually accessible remains measured in tens of thousands of dollars and years of scaling up.

The man ordered his beer. He told his son he loved him.

Somewhere else, another parent stayed silent.

The gap between those two lives is the distance between what is technically possible and what we as a society are willing to pay for.

Hopefully we get to the point soon that anyone, anywhere with the same or similar affliction, if they want to, gets to order that beer.

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

What Having a Voice Gives Here’s what’s interesting: the first thing the man did with his brain implant was ordinary, not profound. He ordered a beer, requested a song he liked, and told his kid something nice. He didn’t write poetry or solve math problems or make some grand statement about the human condition. He just went about his day saying everyday things that people with working vocal cords do without giving it a second thought. We’ve built technology sophisticated enough to read thoughts. More importantly, we’ve built technology capable of restoring the tiny, boring, necessary transactions that make up a life. We’ve helped restore a piece of their humanity. How to Read Between the Lines We probably all know someone who could benefit from the rapid advancements of medicine. If you feel like you don’t, just wait long enough, and unfortunately you will. When you see news about a medical breakthrough, whether it’s brain implants or AI-powered diagnosis or any other advancement, there are questions we need to keep in mind: Where is this being done? Research trials happen at major academic medical centers. If the announcement comes from a handful of institutions, that tells you geography still matters. The technology might be available at University of Geneva or Johns Hopkins, but it’s probably not ready to be picked up at your local CVS. If you don’t live near a center running trials, your access timeline could be measured in years, not months. What’s the current cost, and what are companies promising? Pay attention to the difference between “costs $100,000 now” and “will cost $1,000 at scale.” Companies are usually honest and up front about both numbers, but media coverage tends to lead with the optimistic future price. “At scale” is code for “when we’ve ramped up to doing thousands of these procedures a year and paid back our investors,” so five or ten years away is not an unreasonable estimate, even if it seems around the corner. The current cost is what matters if you or someone you love needs this now. What does “FDA approval” actually mean? Breakthrough device designation, investigational device exemption, “human” trials, and full FDA approval are different stages with different implications. Early approvals mean trials are allowed to proceed. Full approval means it can be marketed during your reality television marathon. Neither tells you whether insurance will cover it. Who’s paying in the current trials? If research grants are covering costs, that tells you the commercial infrastructure doesn’t exist yet. If insurance is covering some patients, that gives you a lens into what the reimbursement battles will look like. If patients are self-paying, that will tell you that the funding isn’t there and it may never come to market. What’s the timeline from trials to general availability? Medical technology follows a different path than drug approval. Even after FDA approval, it can take years to train surgeons, establish protocols, build manufacturing capacity, and negotiate insurance coverage. For complex interventions like brain implants, add more years for the necessary infrastructure to be installed. What conditions need to be true for insurance to approve coverage? This is often the most important question and the one least likely to be answered during announcements on the evening news. Insurance companies make coverage decisions based on evidence of medical necessity, cost-effectiveness compared to existing treatments and assistance, and whether the procedure is considered experimental. Insurers will only approve if it makes sense to their bottom line. Ask yourself these questions and you’ll have a more realistic picture of the space between “this new tech is cool” and “this is available for me right now.” What This Means for Everyone Else Most people reading this will never need a brain-computer interface. But the economics of this technology preview how we’re going to make decisions about who gets access to augmentation and enhancement as these advancements scale up. Who gets to keep their voice when disease or tragedy takes it? Who gets to communicate with their child? Who’s deciding what the threshold is for “worth the cost”? The basic framework applies to any medical breakthrough you or someone you care about might face. What assistive or medical technology exists NOW that insurance actually covers? Who owns the data from any device they use? How is consent handled as their capacity changes? Who makes decisions about what equipment they get access to? What’s the realistic timeline for new treatments to become available outside research trials? What’s the actual cost if you had to self-pay? These questions aren’t hypothetical. Families are dealing with them right now, every day, and mostly in isolation without the advocacy and resources that are typically needed. The Last Mile Brain-computer interfaces are being sold to us as the next frontier. Merge with AI to be smarter. Upload your consciousness to live forever. All the sci-fi stuff, seemingly within reach. But before we get to any of that, we have to solve a more basic problem: letting someone who’s locked in their own body, unable to move, tell their kid “I love my cool son.” The man who was diagnosed with ALS in 2015, who got his implant in 2019, who finally managed to communicate in 2022 after several months of failed attempts, got that dignity back. His son will know his dad found a way to talk to him, even when ALS took everything else. That’s worth celebrating. But whether you or a loved one gets that chance still depends on living near the right research center, fitting the right trial criteria, being in the right place at the right time. And even as the technology improves and companies promise affordability is coming, the gap between what’s technically possible and what’s actually accessible remains measured in tens of thousands of dollars and years of scaling up. The man ordered his beer. He told his son he loved him. Somewhere else, another parent stayed silent. The gap between those two lives is the distance between what is technically possible and what we as a society are willing to pay for. Hopefully we get to the point soon that anyone, anywhere with the same or similar affliction, if they want to, gets to order that beer. Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset. If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.

item.title: Your Best-Converting Customer Might Not Be Human
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
Your Best-Converting Customer Might Not Be Human

The challenge? Now it’s your job to figure out what it wants.

Image created in ChatGPT

Your Black Friday dashboard looked great this year. Sales up, conversion up, record traffic. Then someone on your team pointed at a line item everyone has been taking a glance at and then mostly ignoring: “AI-driven traffic.”

According to Adobe Analytics, that line is no longer background noise. Compared to last year, AI-driven traffic jumped 805%, and Adobe’s October data says those shoppers were 16% more likely to convert than everyone else.

Cool, right? Free money from the future!

Well, not exactly. Just wait until someone asks the only question that matters: who is that traffic, and what did they actually see before they bought from you?

If you run an e-commerce site or are in charge of growing one, congratulations, you just inherited a new channel that behaves absolutely nothing like the customers you just spent a decade learning to optimize for.

And unlike every other traffic source you’ve dealt with, this one doesn’t care about your homepage, your brand story, or your clever funnel. It just follows links.

And you thought you still sold to people? Not in this economy.

What AI-Driven Traffic Actually Looks Like

Let’s decode what’s actually happening behind those numbers.

AI-driven traffic isn’t one thing. It’s a mix of answer engines like Perplexity deep-linking into your product pages, browser copilots that build comparison charts across ten competitors, shopping plugins that autofill carts based on prompts, and retailer chatbots that guide people straight to checkout. Some of this traffic comes from humans using AI tools. Some of it comes from AI tools doing reconnaissance on behalf of humans. And some of it is most assuredly just bots following scripted flows that happen to look like very motivated shoppers.

In your analytics right now, this traffic is more than likely mis-bucketed. It shows up as “direct” because the referrer got stripped. Or maybe it shows up under an odd partner UTM that tags the source but doesn’t quite know how to label it. Or you might find it in your “organic” bucket even though no human typed the URL to your website.

The salespeople trying to get you to buy these AI shopping tools love to show you those increased analytics. It proves their value, justifies their fees, and makes them look like growth partners. For you? It’s a blurry mix of real customers, half-autonomous shopping scripts, and weird edge cases that quietly blow up all the assumptions your site was built on.

Your Funnel Was Designed for Humans

As it stands now, your entire site architecture assumes humans start at the top of a funnel.

You drive them to your homepage with ads, social media, etc. They land on your homepage. They browse categories. They read your brand story. They absorb context about shipping, returns, bundles, compatibility, and all of the other perks of your product. They click around until they trust you enough to buy. Then they make the purchase.

AI shoppers skip all of that.

They land on a random product variant page you built and then iterated off of and forgot to delete. They land on an old promo page that still ranks in search. They land on a bundle offer with shipping rules that only make sense if you read three other pages first. Then they bounce, or they buy, or they submit an angry support ticket that starts with “Perplexity said this would ship in two days and now your site says five.”

Translation: you just inherited a bunch of new jobs nobody made plans, or budget room, for.

Nobody’s printing “Head of AI traffic operations” on your org chart yet, but that work has to fall on someone’s lap.

None of this showed up in the Q4 planning meeting. It’s just your problem now.

Your Monday Morning Playbook

Okay. So what’s the actual work to be done here?

First: Segment it. Create an “AI / assistant” traffic segment in your analytics. This isn’t going to be perfect. Some of it will still hide in “direct” or “organic.” But you can group every known AI referrer, every partner tool, and every piece of suspiciously high-intent traffic that doesn’t fit the normal patterns. You need visibility before you can manage it.

Second: Take note of the weirdness. Track the useful stuff for this segment specifically: conversion rate, average order value, refund and return rates, and support tickets. In other words, all the places AI can quietly turn “great traffic” into “why has this gotten so expensive?”

Don’t just look at aggregate numbers and assume you’re doing better because the AI traffic is driving more clicks. Even if it’s increasing your conversions you still want to track if it’s hurting your second sale or upsell. There’s still a human making human decisions on the other side of that transaction. It’s just that you now have AI acting as the middleman.

Third: Click it like an agent. Consumers are slowly coming around to using AI agents to do the work for them. You should prepare for that. Go into Perplexity, ChatGPT, Gemini, or whatever other tools your customers are likely using, and run test prompts about your own products. Ask them what they recommend. Ask them about shipping times, return policies, compatibility, etc. and see what comes back. See which pages they link to. See what copy they quote. Maybe most importantly, see what promises they make on your behalf. Did they get it right or are they hallucinating into making promises your company can’t keep? (Now might be the time for an added disclaimer on your website protecting you from that.) Your market research now includes experiencing the same “pre-click” search experience your customers are getting.

Fourth: Harden your front doors. Review your landing pages. Your most popular products, your core category pages, your main promo offers. Make sure they’re robust. Updated pricing, clear shipping info, accurate stock levels, and internal consistency across every claim. You can’t control what the AI says, but you can control what it sees when it lands.

Fifth: Start a tiny playbook. Document which pages are safe to deep-link, which promises are fragile or fleeting, and what you’d actually want an AI assistant to say about your store if you had any say in the matter. This may sound like overkill. But when you’re in month three of cleaning up the same bad recommendation over and over, wondering when your job quietly added an “AI damage control” task, you’ll be glad you did it.

You Don’t Just Run a Site Anymore

Here’s the thing not many are saying out loud: you don’t just run an e-commerce site anymore. You run an API for other people’s copilots.

The major platforms are getting to brag about huge jumps in traffic growth while you get the unpriced work. You’re now the end-user getting to debug misaligned agents, smoothing over bad recommendations. You’re the one absorbing customer frustration when an AI over-promises and under-delivers.

The real new job here is AI traffic operations, whether anyone wants to call it that or not. You now own what these agents see, what they say, and what they do in your store, even though you don’t control their interface, their training data, or their decision logic (and you certainly aren’t getting a share in their profits).

***

You’ve spent your career learning how to market to humans. You A/B tested headlines, optimized loading speeds, refined your funnel again and again. You built a brand. Now your fastest-growing customer segment doesn’t read your homepage, doesn’t see the beautiful graphic design you paid extra for, and doesn’t care about your clever three-step upsell checkout flow.

All it does is follow links. And when those links break, when the promises don’t match, when the experience doesn’t land, that’s money left on the table (and probably being scooped up by a competitor).

Welcome to AI traffic operations. Nobody told you it was part of the job description, but it showed up on your dashboard anyway. You can ignore the fancy new title if you want. The work is already yours.

Greg Dessau writes about AI, work, and attention at Human Offset.

If you’re wrestling with how to actually implement AI in your organization, feel free to reach out.
