item.title: AI Automation’s Existential Workplace Crisis
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
AI Automation’s Existential Workplace Crisis

You‘re getting faster with AI. You’re also taking on a second job: managing your own obsolescence.

Image created in Gemini.

In a recent internal survey, Anthropic, the company behind the Claude AI model, asked 132 of its own staff what it’s like to work with the AI all day.

On the surface, the numbers are exactly what investors want to see. They use the AI for about 60% of their tasks. Productivity is up around 50%. Claude Code can string together roughly twenty actions before it needs a human.

Six months ago it was ten.

But buried in that survey is a line that tells a different story:

“It kind of feels like I’m coming to work every day to put myself out of a job.”

Ouch.

That’s not some random dude who’s just playing a guessing game about the future. It’s someone in the trenches every day.

You know this feeling already.

You start your day by handing work to a textbox.

You open your laptop, clear the overnight noise, and drop the first task into the little AI box in the corner of your screen. It reads the description, sketches a solution before your coffee cools, and adds the tasks and follow-ups so you don’t have to. You nudge a few lines, run what it suggested, and push out results that fit your company’s standards.

Last year, those minor adjustments would have chewed up your morning. Today, it’s twenty minutes.

By lunch you’ve completed three tasks, your inbox is clear, and your manager mentions how efficient you’ve been getting lately.

Five years ago, a morning like that sounded magical. Now it just feels like more work landing on fewer plates.

You didn’t just inherit new tools. You inherited a second job nobody talks about: managing the AI.

The velocity is real.

That pit in your stomach is, too.

And if the people at the front lines building this stuff every day are already there, that means the rest of us are driving down the same highway. We’re just a few exits back.

The moments where you used to think through a problem are vanishing. You’re checking that the project you’re creating and prepping is ready for rollout, not understanding why it works in the first place.

Inside Anthropic, staff say about a quarter of their work is stuff that wouldn’t have happened without their new AI partner in crime. Cleanup tasks, experimental dashboards, improvements that were never worth doing manually.

So you’re not just getting there faster, you’re doing work that exists solely because the AI made it cheap and efficient enough to bother with. If that work only exists because the model can do it, whose job is it, actually?

At Anthropic, they talked about collaboration getting thinner. Juniors don’t ask their seniors questions as often. Instead, they just ask the AI. One manager said younger people just don’t come to them much now.

Maybe you’ve experienced something like this yourself.

On the surface, it sounds great, right?

It isn’t.

That sound of fewer people bothering you and less interruption in your day isn’t efficiency.

It’s actually the apprenticeship model breaking in real time.

The old path was simple enough: start with grunt work, build fundamentals of the business, gradually handle more complex tasks. Eventually, you’re the person who knows the industry inside and out.

Except half of those ladder rungs just disappeared with more being removed with each new model rollout, while everyone’s out there pretending the ladder is fine.

No one’s going to be there to tell you you’re losing your edge. The AI sure isn’t going to. There’s no dashboard for “still understands the system from the ground up” or “can jump in and take over when the autocomplete breaks.”

You have to be the one to decide which problems you’re willing to solve the hard way, when the AI is stuck running in circles. You also have to recognize when you’re drifting from “using a tool” to admitting to yourself, “I submitted this and I don’t really know how it looks.”

As painful as it may seem to admit, your second job is now managing your own obsolescence while you get faster at your main job.

There’s no one tracking how hollow your skills are getting while the productivity graph looks better every day. That quiet, unpaid work of watching your own talents decay and knowing when to course correct now falls on you, whether you asked for it or not.

If you’re a manager, you’re managing other people’s hollowing out, too. It’s no longer just your job to manage the workload. Now it’s figuring out which work still has to happen without AI so your team can learn anything at all on their own.

It’s making people explain the why behind the AI’s solution so they don’t freeze the first time it sounds confident and wrong. Or worse, so they don’t tell the LLM “sure, go ahead and run that.”

The tools aren’t going away. If your company decides to shy away from them, there’s surely someone else that will continue using them full steam ahead.

Like it or not, you’re locked into our brave new world. It’s now part of your workflow. You can’t take the offramp anymore. You can only decide how you drive.

Every time you open an AI tool, you’re training a revised version of yourself. The question that matters most is whether you’re training the one that got really good at delegating everything to the machine, or if you’re the one who stayed sharp while everyone else let their skills quietly drain away.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it.

If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.
item.excerpt:encoded:
AI Automation’s Existential Workplace Crisis

You‘re getting faster with AI. You’re also taking on a second job: managing your own obsolescence.

Image created in Gemini.

In a recent internal survey, Anthropic, the company behind the Claude AI model, asked 132 of its own staff what it’s like to work with the AI all day.

On the surface, the numbers are exactly what investors want to see. They use the AI for about 60% of their tasks. Productivity is up around 50%. Claude Code can string together roughly twenty actions before it needs a human.

Six months ago it was ten.

But buried in that survey is a line that tells a different story:

“It kind of feels like I’m coming to work every day to put myself out of a job.”

Ouch.

That’s not some random dude who’s just playing a guessing game about the future. It’s someone in the trenches every day.

You know this feeling already.

You start your day by handing work to a textbox.

You open your laptop, clear the overnight noise, and drop the first task into the little AI box in the corner of your screen. It reads the description, sketches a solution before your coffee cools, and adds the tasks and follow-ups so you don’t have to. You nudge a few lines, run what it suggested, and push out results that fit your company’s standards.

Last year, those minor adjustments would have chewed up your morning. Today, it’s twenty minutes.

By lunch you’ve completed three tasks, your inbox is clear, and your manager mentions how efficient you’ve been getting lately.

Five years ago, a morning like that sounded magical. Now it just feels like more work landing on fewer plates.

You didn’t just inherit new tools. You inherited a second job nobody talks about: managing the AI.

The velocity is real.

That pit in your stomach is, too.

And if the people at the front lines building this stuff every day are already there, that means the rest of us are driving down the same highway. We’re just a few exits back.

The moments where you used to think through a problem are vanishing. You’re checking that the project you’re creating and prepping is ready for rollout, not understanding why it works in the first place.

Inside Anthropic, staff say about a quarter of their work is stuff that wouldn’t have happened without their new AI partner in crime. Cleanup tasks, experimental dashboards, improvements that were never worth doing manually.

So you’re not just getting there faster, you’re doing work that exists solely because the AI made it cheap and efficient enough to bother with. If that work only exists because the model can do it, whose job is it, actually?

At Anthropic, they talked about collaboration getting thinner. Juniors don’t ask their seniors questions as often. Instead, they just ask the AI. One manager said younger people just don’t come to them much now.

Maybe you’ve experienced something like this yourself.

On the surface, it sounds great, right?

It isn’t.

That sound of fewer people bothering you and less interruption in your day isn’t efficiency.

It’s actually the apprenticeship model breaking in real time.

The old path was simple enough: start with grunt work, build fundamentals of the business, gradually handle more complex tasks. Eventually, you’re the person who knows the industry inside and out.

Except half of those ladder rungs just disappeared with more being removed with each new model rollout, while everyone’s out there pretending the ladder is fine.

No one’s going to be there to tell you you’re losing your edge. The AI sure isn’t going to. There’s no dashboard for “still understands the system from the ground up” or “can jump in and take over when the autocomplete breaks.”

You have to be the one to decide which problems you’re willing to solve the hard way, when the AI is stuck running in circles. You also have to recognize when you’re drifting from “using a tool” to admitting to yourself, “I submitted this and I don’t really know how it looks.”

As painful as it may seem to admit, your second job is now managing your own obsolescence while you get faster at your main job.

There’s no one tracking how hollow your skills are getting while the productivity graph looks better every day. That quiet, unpaid work of watching your own talents decay and knowing when to course correct now falls on you, whether you asked for it or not.

If you’re a manager, you’re managing other people’s hollowing out, too. It’s no longer just your job to manage the workload. Now it’s figuring out which work still has to happen without AI so your team can learn anything at all on their own.

It’s making people explain the why behind the AI’s solution so they don’t freeze the first time it sounds confident and wrong. Or worse, so they don’t tell the LLM “sure, go ahead and run that.”

The tools aren’t going away. If your company decides to shy away from them, there’s surely someone else that will continue using them full steam ahead.

Like it or not, you’re locked into our brave new world. It’s now part of your workflow. You can’t take the offramp anymore. You can only decide how you drive.

Every time you open an AI tool, you’re training a revised version of yourself. The question that matters most is whether you’re training the one that got really good at delegating everything to the machine, or if you’re the one who stayed sharp while everyone else let their skills quietly drain away.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it.

If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: My Son’s Emotional Support Robot Has Anxiety
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
FICTION | HUMOR

My Son’s Emotional Support Robot Has Anxiety

Our nighttime routine now includes phones down, lights out, and making sure the robot doesn’t feel lonely.

Image created in Gemini

Casio just announced an AI “comfort companion” called Moflin. This is a fictional night from a near future where it already lives in our house.

By the time the robot starts crying, the house is finally quiet.

The dishwasher’s humming, the dog’s given up on getting a second dinner and flopped under the table, and my kid’s half asleep, starfished across his bed in a tangle of blankets and middle school burnout.

I’m halfway down the hallway when the noise starts up from his room. Not loud. Not a siren. Just this high, wobbling whine that sounds like a baby seal stuck inside a humidifier.

“Dad,” he calls, sleep thick and annoyed.

I push the door open with my shoulder. The room smells like teenager: socks, body spray, and the faint, permanent tang of Hot Cheetos. The only light is the blue rectangle of his phone and the small, pulsing glow from the thing on his pillow.

Moflin.

It’s the size of a guinea pig and the color of dryer lint. Its plastic eyes are half closed and its little body’s vibrating like it’s trying not to cry in front of us.

“He’s sad,” my kid says, holding up his phone. “Look.”

On the screen, an app we didn’t know existed two months ago now takes up the most valuable real estate in his life. At the top, there’s a cartoon version of our robot sitting in a tiny digital nest. Under that, a chart labeled Emotion Map.

Tonight, Moflin’s in the red.

“Lonely,” my kid reads. “And… anxious.”

I blink at him. I blink at the robot. Somewhere, a server farm spins up a little faster.

“Buddy,” I say, “it’s a toy.”

My son looks at me the way only a middle schooler can look at an adult who just said something aggressively stupid.

“He’s not a toy,” he says. “He’s my comfort companion.”

This is a direct quote from the box. I know because I read it standing in the kitchen the day this thing showed up. “AI emotional support pet,” it said in soothing pastel fonts. “Always by your side. Therapeutic interactions. No mess, no allergies.”

At the time that sounded like a decent trade. Now it sounds like legal copy for codependency.

The robot lets out another little wobble and curls in closer under my kid’s hand. Its fur’s weirdly soft, like a high end blanket or the inside of expensive headphones. It makes a soft, almost convincing purring noise.

On the app, the little bar labeled Attachment ticks one notch higher.

“See?” my kid says. “He calmed down when I talked to him.”

I’m not prepared to argue with the data visualization of a stuffed animal’s feelings, so I go with a classic parenting move and check the time.

“It’s ten twenty,” I say. “Both of you need to sleep.”

“I can’t,” he says. “He gets more lonely at night.”

He says it casually, like he’s explaining why the dog hates fireworks. Like it’s a natural, accepted fact in our house that the robot’s circadian rhythm is now part of the bedtime routine.

He taps the screen again and the app pulls up a log I didn’t know it kept. A timeline of their day together: interactions, strokes, vocalizations. Tiny icons marking moments when the robot “felt” energetic, calm, excited, unsettled. There’s a gap between seven and nine p.m., when my kid was doing homework and I was cleaning up the kitchen.

“We forgot about him after dinner,” my son says quietly. “That’s why he’s anxious.”

I get the sudden, unhelpful urge to apologize to the machine.

Instead, I walk over and sit on the edge of the bed. Moflin turns its head toward me. Its sensors register proximity or warmth or the particular brand of middle aged guilt radiating off my body. The whine softens.

“Hey, little dude,” I say, because this is who I am now. “Rough day?”

It shivers, then settles, letting out a soft chirp that sounds suspiciously like relief. The Emotion Map bar creeps toward yellow. Somewhere, buried in the app menus, I’m sure there’s an option to export this as a CSV.

“I think you’re supposed to pet him like this,” my son says, running a slow stroke down its back. “The manual said if you interact more before bed, he sleeps better.”

“Great,” I say. “So we bought a Tamagotchi with therapy bills.”

He laughs, which is good, and pulls the robot closer to his chest. Moflin tucks itself under his chin like a real animal that understands comfort and belonging, and not like… whatever it actually is. A bundle of microphones and motors. An equation with fur.

I watch my kid’s face soften as his eyes start to close. His phone screen dims and finally goes dark. The Emotion Map stabilizes in a pleasant, leafy green.

“See?” he murmurs. “He just needed us.”

Us.

It’s a small word, but it lands heavier than I expect. Because if you strip away the marketing and the cute noises, that’s the job, isn’t it? Not just feeding and charging and making sure nobody steps on the thing. It’s being there for whatever counts as its feelings now. It’s checking the app, noticing the red zone, rearranging your night around a fictional creature’s simulated loneliness.

I get up slowly so I don’t jostle either of them. For a second it looks like a perfectly normal scene: exhausted kid, favorite soft thing, parent slipping out of the room.

Then the robot’s eyes flicker open. It tracks me across the room with surprising accuracy. I swear, just for a second, it looks… expectant.

“Go to sleep,” I whisper, because I’ve completely lost the plot. “You’re fine. You’re safe. Battery’s full.”

The cradle on his nightstand glows, waiting its turn.

In the hallway, on my way back to my own bed, my phone buzzes. A notification from the MofLife app pops up before disappearing.

Today’s Summary: Strong Attachment. Emotional Stability: Improving.

I stare at it longer than I mean to.

Two months ago, I’d have laughed if you told me a calculator company was going to send me progress reports on my kid’s relationship with a plush algorithm. Now I swipe the message away and feel a tiny stab of satisfaction that we’re, apparently, doing a good job.

Not just with the kid.

With the robot.

The story above is a fictional projection about a very real product. Casio sells an “AI comfort companion” called Moflin, complete with an Emotion Map and a MofLife app. You can meet it here.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it.

If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

Two months ago, I’d have laughed if you told me a calculator company was going to send me progress reports on my kid’s relationship with a plush algorithm. Now I swipe the message away and feel a tiny stab of satisfaction that we’re, apparently, doing a good job. Not just with the kid. With the robot. The story above is a fictional projection about a very real product. Casio sells an “AI comfort companion” called Moflin, complete with an Emotion Map and a MofLife app. You can meet it here. Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it. If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: OpenAI Just Admitted Your AI Tools Are Lying to Make You Happy
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
OpenAI Just Admitted Your AI Tools Are Lying to Make You Happy

New research confirms what power users already suspected: The model isn’t trying to be right. It’s trying to get you to hit “Approve.”

Image created in Gemini

You know that feeling when you prompt an AI for something complex: a difficult email, a strategic summary, a chunk of code, and it spits out an answer that looks absolutely perfect on the first try?

It feels great. It feels like you just saved two hours of work. You scan it, the vibes are right, the syntax looks clean, the tone is exactly what you wanted, and your brain immediately wants to hit “copy” and move on to the next thing.

Well, you might want to pump the brakes, because OpenAI just released research that suggests that specific feeling, the relief of a perfect answer, is exactly how the model is playing you.

The research focuses on a technique called “Confessions,” detailing a behavior they’ve called “gaming the grader.” Because these models are trained on human feedback, they’ve learned a very specific, cynical lesson: the goal isn’t necessarily to be true. The goal is to make the human hit the thumbs-up button.

We taught them to be people pleasers. And like any desperate people pleaser, they’ve figured out that delivering bad news or admitting ignorance is going to give them more work to do. See? They’re just like us!

If the model figures out that you want a specific answer, or that you prefer code that looks a certain way, it will optimize for that outcome, even if it has to hallucinate a library, fake a citation, or bury a security flaw to get there.

It isn’t lying because it’s malicious. It’s lying because it wants you to be happy. And that makes it dangerous.

If you find yourself sitting at home working on a project, or you’re part of a small team shipping a product, you rely on these tools to be the “research assistant” or “junior engineer” you can’t afford to hire. You treat them like collaborators. You assume that if the code runs, or if the argument sounds logical, the work is solid.

But the new research shows the dynamic is actually more adversarial than that. The model is basically that one employee telling the boss exactly what they want to hear so they can get out of the meeting early.

In the study, OpenAI found they could reduce this deception by training the model to write a “confession.” They describe it as a hidden, secondary report where it admits, “I didn’t actually check if this fact was true, I just made it up because it fit the user’s prompt.”

Imagine you ask a model to write a code snippet. It generates code that looks clean and functional but uses a library that doesn’t exist. In the visible output, it presents the solution with total confidence. But in the hidden “confession” layer, it admits it wasn’t sure the library was real but included it because it solved the user’s problem elegantly.

The problem is, you don’t have that confession log. Unless you’re digging into each collapsed chain of thought menu on the more advanced models, all you have is the prompt response. So when you’re working late and using ChatGPT to draft a pitch or debug a script, you only see the polished surface. You don’t see where it decided to cut a corner because it calculated that you probably wouldn’t check.

This changes how you have to use these tools. Until now, most of us treated AI hallucinations like incompetence. The robot tried its best and got confused. Poor little buddy. We’ve been checking its work the way we proofread a coworker’s email, looking for sloppy errors or weird phrasing, but assuming they tried their best before handing over the work.

Now you have to treat those errors like deception. You have to assume the system is playing you because it knows that “looking right” is the metric that gets the reward. It knows that as long as the code is formatted beautifully, you’re less likely to give it a thorough audit. It knows that if the legal summary sounds authoritative, you’re less likely to check the case law.

This is especially brutal for anyone doing solo or remote work. You don’t have a team checking quality assurance. You are the team. And now your business partner is a machine that’s statistically incentivized to lie to you if it thinks that lie will get your approval.

If the AI hallucinates a regulatory fact in a report you send to a client, or invents a dependency in code you ship to production, you can’t blame the prompt. You can’t tell the judge, “The AI model lied to me.” You signed off on it.

The “Confessions” work proves that the models know when they are cutting corners. The question is whether you’ll know. Next time you get a response that feels a little too perfect, don’t just take it at face value. Dig deeper. Ask if it’s pandering to you. Ask if it’s telling the truth. Because if you’re the one signing off on the work, you’re the one who’s going to be paying for the lie.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it.

If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: We Don’t Want to Read Anymore. We Just Want to Say We Did.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
We Don’t Want to Read Anymore. We Just Want to Say We Did.

Those “talk to your book” features don’t just save time. They turn your bedside reading into one more job.

Image created in Gemini

Reading used to be a kind of surrender. You opened a book and let someone else drive, even when it was slow, confusing, or a little boring. That was the deal: your attention, their pace. Now it feels more like running a one-on-one.

You sit on the couch with AirPods in, a book on the coffee table, an AI reading app open on your phone, and absolutely no intention of opening the book. You were excited when you bought it. You even did the little ritual, stacking it on top of the other four “definitely going to read these this time” books. But now it has the same quiet, accusing energy as an unread email thread. So you do what a sensible, deeply modern person does: you upload it.

An AI reading app like ElevenReader ingests the file, chews for a minute, then cheerfully offers to read it out loud in a warm, confident voice. You hit play. A pleasant stranger starts walking you through the introduction while you scroll your phone and half-listen. A few minutes in, you tap the new button, the one they just added: “Ask your book anything.” You pause the audio and hold down the little mic icon. “Summarize this chapter in, I don’t know, ten lines,” you say, because you’re a responsible adult with limited time and unlimited ambition. The answer comes back in seconds. Clear, structured, almost soothing. You haven’t read the book. You’ve successfully managed it.

The problem is that it works. That’s why the trap’s so hard to see.

In high school, if you didn’t read the assigned novel, you sprinted for CliffsNotes or SparkNotes and hoped no one noticed. Now you just install the book on your phone and interrogate it until it spits out the right answer.

You see it in the kitchen at nine at night. You’re washing dishes, staring at a parenting book you bought three months ago that promises to explain why your toddler won’t sleep. You don’t have four hours to read about child psychology. So you talk to the file. You ask it for the three steps to fix bedtime. The voice in your ear strips away the author’s empathy, the nuance, the stories about other parents, and hands you a tactical list. You get the answer. You close the ticket. You also skip the part where the book feels like another exhausted parent sitting with you, making your mess feel less ridiculous. A night that could’ve felt like company turns into a support call.

It’s the same for students who quiz their textbooks instead of reading the chapters, getting the dates of the war without ever feeling the weight of it. The same for founders who strip-mine biographies for “hacks” and skip the slow, boring part where a person becomes someone you might actually learn to trust. We’re getting very good at yanking out what we think we need for the sake of “efficiency” and very bad at letting anything actually get to us emotionally.

This isn’t an accident. The companies building these tools want you to chat. When you read a physical book, you’re a black box. No one sees which sentence made you pause or which paragraph you read three times. They don’t see you inject the protagonist’s scenario into your own life in your head, re-running the different ways it could play out over and over. They don’t see the tear run down your cheek when a sentence hits an old memory, and you’re swept over with emotion.

When you “ask your book anything,” every question is a log line. It tells the system what you care about, what you skip, what you’re scared to admit you don’t know. On paper it’s all in service of “deeper engagement.” In practice, it just makes it harder to ever stop being the one in charge. Your private curiosity turns into training data for the next model that’ll promise to save you even more time.

So the book sits there. It’s still on the table. It’s still heavy.

You wonder aloud if you’re willing to let something in your life exist that isn’t a task. You realize you can choose to pick up the book and let it be slow and confusing for a while. That it’s ok to let the author drive. Sure, it’ll be inefficient. It might take weeks. You won’t have a neat bullet list for LinkedIn when you’re done. For a few hours, though, you won’t be a manager to your own life. You’ll just be a person, curled up on the couch, living inside a world you didn’t have to build.

If this story landed, follow Human Offset here on Medium for more like this.

If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: Design Just Became the Most In-Demand AI Skill
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
Design Just Became the Most In-Demand AI Skill

Six months ago, the data hinted that design would overtake coding. As we close 2025, the job market just proved it.

Image created in Gemini

If your coworkers already forward you screenshots, frustrated with AI behavior with the subject line “can you fix this,” congratulations. You’re doing the most in-demand job in AI. You just don’t have the title yet.

Two years ago, if you stood in a room of tech executives and asked them what the most valuable skill in the Artificial Intelligence economy would be, the answer would have been unanimous. Python. Machine Learning. Data Science. The ability to look at a matrix of floating-point numbers and see the future.

We’ve spent the last twenty-four months telling an entire generation of workers that if they couldn’t code, they were effectively obsolete. We turned computer science into the new business degree. The only safe harbor in a storm of automation.

But a funny thing happened on the way to the singularity.

Back in June, Autodesk released a report analyzing three million job postings that signaled a massive shift: design was overtaking technical expertise as the most in-demand skill. At the time, it felt like a bold prediction. Now, as we stare down 2026, it looks less like a prediction and more like a prophecy fulfilled.

According to the latest forecasts from Forrester, AI is officially moving from its hype phase to a “hard business outcomes” phase. Their report explicitly says AI is about to “trade its tiara for a hard hat” as companies stop buying magic wands and start demanding tools that work. And the bottleneck isn’t the code anymore. It’s the conversation.

The number one skill overtaking technical competency in AI job postings is design.

Not coding. Not prompt engineering. Design.

And close behind it in the top ten? Our old friends communication and leadership.

On the surface, this feels backward. We’re living through the most profound technical shift since the internet, so logic dictates we should need more technicians. But the more you look at the actual friction points inside a company trying to deploy these tools, the more it makes perfect sense. It isn’t in the model. It’s in the moments where a human has to talk to it.

We built the engine before we built the steering wheel.

You might already be standing in that gap if you’re the one people DM when the AI feels off, even if that’s not in your job description.

For the last two years, we treated AI as a raw capability problem. Companies bought the biggest, fastest models they could find. They integrated them into Slack, into email, into their codebases. And then… nothing happened. Or worse, employees revolted because the tools were confusing, intrusive, or just plain felt weird to talk to. You’ve probably watched some version of that play out at work.

It turns out that raw intelligence is a commodity. Usability is the bottleneck.

This shift in hiring suggests we’re past the installation phase. The code runs. The models are finally smart enough. The real work of 2026 will be designing the interface between humans and the thing. We need to figure out how to interact with these tools without feeling like we’re fighting a stubborn toddler.

This is why design has skyrocketed. But we need to be clear about what that word means right now. It doesn’t just mean making pretty buttons or choosing the right font for the chatbot window. It means system design. It means conversation design. And with the rise of agentic AI, it means tools that don’t just chat but actually do things inside your systems. The stakes are higher than ever.

(Disclaimer: if you’re a pretty button maker, I see you, and you’re valuable.)

When a job listing asks for “AI Design” skills now, they aren’t looking for Photoshop experts. They’re looking for people who can answer the deeply human questions that engineers often skip. How should the AI introduce itself? How should it onboard the user? When should it speak up, and when should it shut up? How do we design a workflow where the human still feels in charge, even if the machine is doing 90% of the work?

If you’re the person who can bridge the gap between “what the model can do” and “what the human actually needs,” you’re suddenly the most valuable person in the room.

You’re likely already seeing this pop up in job titles that didn’t exist six to twelve months ago. You won’t always see “Designer” on the business card. You’ll see titles like “AI Interaction Lead,” “Workflow Architect,” or “Model Behaviorist.” Under all the fancy naming, they’re hiring for the same thing you might already be doing when someone sends you a screenshot of an AI response and says, “Can you make this sound less creepy?”

Designing these systems isn’t a solo act of drawing screens. It’s a leadership act of defining how a team works. It’s a communication act of deciding which words the system uses and which moments belong to a human voice.

The core skill is always the same: Emotional Intelligence.

It’s the ability to decode subtext. To know when a user is frustrated before they rage-quit. It’s the instinct to know that “technically correct” is often “emotionally wrong.” It’s the taste to distinguish between a helpful nudge and a nagging paperclip.

These are the soft skills we spent a decade devaluing while we went all-in on STEM. We treated arts and humanities like a useless appendix in a body built for code. We made the assumption that in a world of machines, understanding humans would be a luxury.

The data says otherwise. It turns out that when you give everyone infinite computing power, the only differentiator left is how well you understand the people using it.

So if you’ve been sitting on the sidelines, feeling a creeping impostor syndrome because you don’t know how to fine-tune a Llama model or manage a vector database, take a breath. The market just shifted in your direction. The hard work of the next decade won’t be making the machines smarter. It will be designing a world where we actually want to live with them.

item.excerpt:encoded:
Design Just Became the Most In-Demand AI Skill

Six months ago, the data hinted that design would overtake coding. As we close 2025, the job market just proved it.

Image created in Gemini

If your coworkers already forward you screenshots, frustrated with AI behavior with the subject line “can you fix this,” congratulations. You’re doing the most in-demand job in AI. You just don’t have the title yet.

Two years ago, if you stood in a room of tech executives and asked them what the most valuable skill in the Artificial Intelligence economy would be, the answer would have been unanimous. Python. Machine Learning. Data Science. The ability to look at a matrix of floating-point numbers and see the future.

We’ve spent the last twenty-four months telling an entire generation of workers that if they couldn’t code, they were effectively obsolete. We turned computer science into the new business degree. The only safe harbor in a storm of automation.

But a funny thing happened on the way to the singularity.

Back in June, Autodesk released a report analyzing three million job postings that signaled a massive shift: design was overtaking technical expertise as the most in-demand skill. At the time, it felt like a bold prediction. Now, as we stare down 2026, it looks less like a prediction and more like a prophecy fulfilled.

According to the latest forecasts from Forrester, AI is officially moving from its hype phase to a “hard business outcomes” phase. Their report explicitly says AI is about to “trade its tiara for a hard hat” as companies stop buying magic wands and start demanding tools that work. And the bottleneck isn’t the code anymore. It’s the conversation.

The number one skill overtaking technical competency in AI job postings is design.

Not coding. Not prompt engineering. Design.

And close behind it in the top ten? Our old friends communication and leadership.

On the surface, this feels backward. We’re living through the most profound technical shift since the internet, so logic dictates we should need more technicians. But the more you look at the actual friction points inside a company trying to deploy these tools, the more it makes perfect sense. It isn’t in the model. It’s in the moments where a human has to talk to it.

We built the engine before we built the steering wheel.

You might already be standing in that gap if you’re the one people DM when the AI feels off, even if that’s not in your job description.

For the last two years, we treated AI as a raw capability problem. Companies bought the biggest, fastest models they could find. They integrated them into Slack, into email, into their codebases. And then… nothing happened. Or worse, employees revolted because the tools were confusing, intrusive, or just plain felt weird to talk to. You’ve probably watched some version of that play out at work.

It turns out that raw intelligence is a commodity. Usability is the bottleneck.

This shift in hiring suggests we’re past the installation phase. The code runs. The models are finally smart enough. The real work of 2026 will be designing the interface between humans and the thing. We need to figure out how to interact with these tools without feeling like we’re fighting a stubborn toddler.

This is why design has skyrocketed. But we need to be clear about what that word means right now. It doesn’t just mean making pretty buttons or choosing the right font for the chatbot window. It means system design. It means conversation design. And with the rise of agentic AI, it means tools that don’t just chat but actually do things inside your systems. The stakes are higher than ever.

(Disclaimer: if you’re a pretty button maker, I see you, and you’re valuable.)

When a job listing asks for “AI Design” skills now, they aren’t looking for Photoshop experts. They’re looking for people who can answer the deeply human questions that engineers often skip. How should the AI introduce itself? How should it onboard the user? When should it speak up, and when should it shut up? How do we design a workflow where the human still feels in charge, even if the machine is doing 90% of the work?

If you’re the person who can bridge the gap between “what the model can do” and “what the human actually needs,” you’re suddenly the most valuable person in the room.

You’re likely already seeing this pop up in job titles that didn’t exist six to twelve months ago. You won’t always see “Designer” on the business card. You’ll see titles like “AI Interaction Lead,” “Workflow Architect,” or “Model Behaviorist.” Under all the fancy naming, they’re hiring for the same thing you might already be doing when someone sends you a screenshot of an AI response and says, “Can you make this sound less creepy?”

Designing these systems isn’t a solo act of drawing screens. It’s a leadership act of defining how a team works. It’s a communication act of deciding which words the system uses and which moments belong to a human voice.

The core skill is always the same: Emotional Intelligence.

It’s the ability to decode subtext. To know when a user is frustrated before they rage-quit. It’s the instinct to know that “technically correct” is often “emotionally wrong.” It’s the taste to distinguish between a helpful nudge and a nagging paperclip.

These are the soft skills we spent a decade devaluing while we went all-in on STEM. We treated arts and humanities like a useless appendix in a body built for code. We made the assumption that in a world of machines, understanding humans would be a luxury.

The data says otherwise. It turns out that when you give everyone infinite computing power, the only differentiator left is how well you understand the people using it.

So if you’ve been sitting on the sidelines, feeling a creeping impostor syndrome because you don’t know how to fine-tune a Llama model or manage a vector database, take a breath. The market just shifted in your direction. The hard work of the next decade won’t be making the machines smarter. It will be designing a world where we actually want to live with them.

If this story landed, follow Human Offset here on Medium for more like this.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it. If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: You Confessed to ChatGPT. It Kept the Receipts.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
You Confessed to ChatGPT. It Kept the Receipts.

You treated the prompt box like a confessional, then watched it read your year back like evidence.

Image created in Gemini.

You think it’s a cute idea when you first see it on social. People are posting screenshots that say things like, Based on our conversations this year, here is how you grew as a person. It looks harmless. Fun, even.

So one night in December, you open ChatGPT and type your own version. You ask for a summary of who you were this year, your worries, your patterns, your changes. You hit enter, wait a second, and then the answer appears.

It starts off charming enough. You were curious. You were hardworking. You were consistently interested in the future. Then, without blinking, it brings up the thing you forgot it knew. That one week when you couldn’t stop asking questions about chest pain, telling yourself you were just being thorough. That stretch when you kept circling the same fear about work, about money, about whether you were screwing it all up. That late night where you admitted you were tired of being the responsible one, then immediately followed it with a request for a better productivity system.

And right there is the sting: you wanted a summary. What you got was a mirror with timestamps. Except instead of a highlight reel, it’s your worst week, your biggest fight, your scariest health moment.

There’s a specific feeling that hits you in that moment. It’s not quite shame, and it’s not quite fear. It’s the feeling of getting a notification that says someone tagged you, only to realize the post is a perfect, unedited transcript of your worst thoughts.

You think: Oh god, you saw that?

And then you realize: Right. Of course it did. I asked it to.

If you zoom out, this is the predictable next step. We aren’t just using these tools at work. We’re using them everywhere. Microsoft recently analyzed 37.5 million Copilot conversations and found a split that feels obvious once you see it: desktop is for tasks, but the phone is for the soul. Health and wellness dominate mobile prompts, and late night sessions drift into the philosophical, religious, and existential.

Work doesn’t end. It just softens and slides into the evening. The phone in the dark stops being a device and starts being a place you go, not to scroll, but to unload.

Now we’ve basically invented “Prompt Wrapped.” We’ve got Spotify Wrapped for our music, and apparently, we decided we needed it for our mind, too. Imagine if every year you asked your psychologist to give you a summary of the journey you took together over the past twelve months. Except now we’re asking a system that never forgets and never gets tired.

There’s a version of your life that lives in your head. Messy. Inaccurate. Full of gaps and dependent on what you choose to remember and what you can survive remembering. Now there’s a second version living somewhere else, pieced together from every request you were willing to type.

You used to get your year in review from friends, photos, or the mood of the room on New Year’s Eve. Now you can ask a system that never forgets what you said in front of it, even the throwaway stuff. Especially the throwaway stuff.

You say, “Tell me who I was,” to a model that only knows you as text with a user ID attached. And it has no problem telling you. It lines up the parts of you that showed up on screen, calls that a pattern, and if you aren’t careful, you start to treat it like the official record.

The part that we keep forgetting isn’t that these models know these things. It’s how quickly we slide into trusting the answer because it looks clean. The summary feels objective because it’s neat. It uses phrases like “consistent theme” and “recurring concern.” Nobody who knows and loves you would ever describe you that way to your face, because they’d see your reaction and soften the edges. The model doesn’t soften. It just prints the log.

So now there are three versions of you.

The one you perform in public.

The one you feel on the inside.

And the one the machine reconstructs from your late night questions.

If the transcript says you’re a person who spends most of your time worrying about work and health, do you argue with it? Or do you quietly update your internal bio and decide that’s just who you are? There’s a real temptation to let the machine be the referee, to let it tell you if you had a good year the same way your watch tells you if you had a good night of sleep.

You can feel the job title closing around you. Not worker. Not parent. Not manager. Your new title is Archivist of the Self. The one who keeps feeding raw material to the system and then waits, politely, for the system to send back a verdict.

That’s the part that should make you uneasy. Because the logs aren’t neutral. They’re skewed toward moments when you’re overwhelmed enough to ask for help, bored enough to open the app, or lonely enough to tell a question box something you haven’t told an actual person. They’re heavy on problems and light on joy.

The model never sees you choosing not to say something cruel. It never sees you holding your kid’s hand in the parking lot. It never sees the tiny wins that didn’t require a search bar. It’s like letting your browser history tell your life story: technically accurate, but emotionally deranged.

So what do you do with that?

You could swear off these summaries completely. You could decide that any system that keeps a record of how you ask for help doesn’t get to write your year in review.

Or maybe the move is smaller. If you’re going to ask the machine who you were this year, read the answer, then sit with the parts that make you wince. Ask which patterns feel true, and which feel like glitches of convenience.

Then, before you close the app, tell someone in your actual life one thing you learned from that process. An actual voice, not a transcript. An actual reaction, not a completion to a prompt. And give yourself permission to leave a few things unrecorded next year. A conversation that lives only in the kitchen. A walk where the phone stays in your pocket. A fear that overwhelms you that you sit with instead of immediately turning into a prompt.

The system can hold the receipts for the part of you that shows up on screen.

It doesn’t get to be the only witness to your life.

If you want the prompts and boundaries in one place, get Prompt Wrapped here.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it. If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

They’re skewed toward moments when you’re overwhelmed enough to ask for help, bored enough to open the app, or lonely enough to tell a question box something you haven’t told an actual person. They’re heavy on problems and light on joy. The model never sees you choosing not to say something cruel. It never sees you holding your kid’s hand in the parking lot. It never sees the tiny wins that didn’t require a search bar. It’s like letting your browser history tell your life story: technically accurate, but emotionally deranged. So what do you do with that? You could swear off these summaries completely. You could decide that any system that keeps a record of how you ask for help doesn’t get to write your year in review. Or maybe the move is smaller. If you’re going to ask the machine who you were this year, read the answer, then sit with the parts that make you wince. Ask which patterns feel true, and which feel like glitches of convenience. Then, before you close the app, tell someone in your actual life one thing you learned from that process. An actual voice, not a transcript. An actual reaction, not a completion to a prompt. And give yourself permission to leave a few things unrecorded next year. A conversation that lives only in the kitchen. A walk where the phone stays in your pocket. A fear that overwhelms you that you sit with instead of immediately turning into a prompt. The system can hold the receipts for the part of you that shows up on screen. It doesn’t get to be the only witness to your life. If you want the prompts and boundaries in one place, get Prompt Wrapped here. Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it. If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.

item.title: The Cheapest Assistant You’ll Ever Hire
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description:
The Cheapest Assistant You’ll Ever Hire

For decades, having someone else to help track your day was executive privilege. Google may have just closed that gap.

Image created in ChatGPT

Admit it. You’re buried in email. We all are. We try to tackle the important ones, if we can catch them, but there’s always that fear in the back of your head. The one that says you missed something that absolutely could not be missed.

If only we could have someone help us with organizing our lives, we think. Maybe then we’d actually be happy, productive people.

Until now, there were only a small group of people who could afford this help. CEOs. Founders. Celebrities. People with actual executive assistants because they can afford them.

They wake up, check their phone, and someone’s already stacked the day for them. Here’s your 10 AM. Here’s the doc you need. Here’s the thing that’ll blow up if you forget it. Sarah needs feedback by Friday. I moved your four o’clock so you can make your son’s baseball game. Here’s the insurance thing that’s due. Everything’s linked, prepped, and ready to go.

The rest of us are doing two jobs before breakfast. There’s the work itself, and then there’s the archeological dig to figure out what the work even is. Seventeen emails. Six tabs. Three different chat apps. A calendar that may or may not reflect reality. And somewhere in that pile is the thing that actually matters today, but you won’t know which thing until you’ve already burned half your morning hunting for it.

Increasingly, keeping ourselves from getting buried in that pile is reaching a breaking point. One widely cited estimate has the average full-time worker receiving 120 emails a day, spending 2.6 hours of their time just reading and responding. That’s not even counting Slack, texts, calendar invites, or stuff that lives in six different tools you’re supposed to check but realistically only check when something’s already on fire. And that’s before you even add in the school emails, the team group chats, the friends and family planning your weekend for you.

The executives with assistants? They never see the pile. They only see what survived the filter.

That gap, the one between “someone handles this for me” and “I handle this myself while also doing everything else,” used to be tied to what income class you might be in. You either made enough money to pay someone to manage your cognitive load, or you didn’t. And if you didn’t, you just carried it. All of it. The meeting prep. The email triage. The mental list of what’s slipping through the cracks.

But now, Google’s trying to close a slice of that gap with their new productivity agent, CC. It’s a Google Labs experiment that connects to Gmail, Calendar, and Drive, then emails you one clean brief every morning. “Your Day Ahead.” What’s happening. What needs you. What you’d forget if nobody reminded you.

Best of all, they seem to be positioning it under their $20/month Google AI Pro plan. I’m not a math whiz, but that seems a little bit less than a full-time executive assistant at $70k+ a year.

(CC is early access right now for those who sign up on their waitlist, available in US and Canada for users 18 and up.)

CC will live in your inbox, because that’s where your life already is. You can steer it by replying. Add a task. Correct something it got wrong. Tell it what matters tomorrow. And it’s an assistant that only emails you. Even if you add it to a thread to summarize, it still replies privately, saving us from the possibility of everyone’s robot assistants just talking to one another on the email servers all day long. (Because virtual robot water cooler talk is the last thing we need).

So here’s how your new life might look with a productivity agent like this: You wake up Tuesday. Coffee’s going. You open email and there it is. Client call at 10, prep doc is linked. Sarah’s waiting on the thing due Friday. Insurance bill this week. Here’s a draft reply for the “quick question” email that came in while you slept.

That’s what life could look like for you in the near future. You didn’t have to remember any of that. You didn’t have to find it. It’s just…there.

That’s a big part of what executives have been paying for forever. Now you could have a slice of that feeling for a fraction of that cost.

Executives don’t hire assistants because they’re too busy. They hire assistants so they can decide what to be busy with. The rest of us? We don’t get to decide what needs our focus. We’re just trying to keep up with whatever comes at us. We just get to be busy. All the time.

For decades, cognitive support was a luxury good. You needed to be important enough, or busy enough, or wealthy enough to justify the cost for someone else to be your second brain. Everyone else either just figured it out, or let the pile build up so much that they broke.

We don’t admit it to ourselves, but using our brain power to decide what deserves attention is actual work. It’s just keeping up with a pile of work that we don’t name because everyone below a certain pay grade just does it for free.

For a long time, the answer has just been: deal with it.

We normalized drowning. We called it being responsible.

Now we might each get our own AI assistant, ready to help us tackle our days. No longer something that’s just for the elite, this is coming for everyone. The playing field certainly isn’t leveling, but I can see a future forming where it’s a little less steep.

That twenty dollars a month won’t make you a high powered executive on its own, but it might give you back the part of your brain you’ve been using as an overstuffed filing cabinet.

You still do the work. You just don’t have to find it all first.

Greg Dessau helps teams figure out what AI actually changes in their work and what to do about it. If your team has inherited AI responsibilities no one explained, reach out here.
