item.title: When AI Gets Cheap, You Get Expensive
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: Everyone loves a price war until they realize they’re part of the price
item.excerpt:encoded: Everyone loves a price war until they realize they’re part of the price
item.content:encoded:
Everyone loves a price war until they realize they’re part of the price

Image generated with AI. Which feels appropriate, given the subject.

The cost of running AI has dropped roughly tenfold every year since OpenAI launched their large language model turned everybody’s favorite therapist, ChatGPT.

Models that used to require millions of dollars and massive data centers can now run on hardware you’d find in a college computer lab.

Open-source alternatives out of China and Europe are doing what ChatGPT does for a fraction of the price, and new ones keep showing up faster than anyone can keep track of.

So what, right?

Well, the difference isn’t in what it can do, but what it costs.

OpenAI reportedly spent more than $100 million to train GPT-4, and that was two years and several models ago. A Chinese lab called DeepSeek built a competitive model for under $6 million.

Others are doing it for even less.

Some of these models are free to download and use, though governments are already scrambling to restrict which ones and where. The details of who built what matter less than the trend line: the cost of digital intelligence is in free fall, and it’s not slowing down.

That kind of news gets celebrated in tech circles as a win for innovation. But there’s another way to read it, and it has nothing to do with which company built the better product.

Take a moment to think about your boss. Not a hypothetical one, like a Silicon Valley visionary or a Wall Street monopoly man. No, your actual boss. The one who sighs when reviewing quarterly expenses, who has a spreadsheet somewhere tracking headcount against revenue, who has been hearing about AI for two years and thinking, yeah, maybe someday, but it’s expensive and complicated and we’re doing fine.

That excuse is quickly evaporating.

When the cost of running AI drops by a factor of ten or twenty or forty, the math changes for every business that employs people to do knowledge work. Heck, at those prices the calculation changes for the employees doing the gruntwork, too.

And not “eventually” like in some theoretical future. Right now. This quarter. In the budget meeting that’s right around the corner.

The question stops being “can AI do this work and do I want to pay to figure out how to implement it?” and starts being “why am I still paying someone $60,000 a year to do work that costs me essentially nothing?”

This isn’t an “evil boss” kind of question. It’s a responsible one. If you ran a business and someone told you that a task costing five thousand dollars a month could now be done for twelve dollars, you’d at least look into it.

Right?

But as much as it may seem like it’s the responsible thing to do from a business standpoint, it still kind of feels icky, doesn’t it?

This math that makes sense is the kind of math that keeps finance and budget departments happy and workers unemployed.

It’s the kind of math that has never once cared about your mortgage payment or your kid’s braces or the fact that you’re really, really good at your job.

And it’s the kind of math that once it hits critical mass and everyone is crunching the same numbers, to paraphrase Smash Mouth, the layoffs start comin’ and they don’t stop comin’.

One of the scariest parts isn’t that AI is brilliant. It’s that businesses don’t need brilliant. They need good enough, consistently, at scale, for as cheap as the market will allow. And we just crossed that line. The AI doesn’t need to be perfect at your job. It just needs to be close enough that your salary stops making sense to the person writing the checks. Good enough is the enemy of your employment.

You might remember when DeepSeek’s last model dropped in early 2025 and the stock market briefly panicked, wiping nearly $600 billion off Nvidia in a single day. Turns out, that was a tremor. What’s been coming out since is what the tremor was warning us about.

But the sky doesn’t fall all at once. It falls in pieces. One job here. Two jobs there. A hiring freeze that becomes permanent. An intern position that gets quietly deleted. A freelance job offer that never gets refreshed. The sky falls slowly enough that you don’t notice until you’re already buried.

Even OpenAI sees the writing on the wall if you read between the lines. They’ve started exploring ad-supported models now, the same playbook that every tech product falls back on when it can’t charge what it used to.

When the company that convinced everyone to pay premium prices for ChatGPT when Google was still free starts thinking about showing you banner ads, they’re telling you they’re not optimistic about the future of the revenue streams from their paid plans. Why? They’re preparing for a world where AI costs almost nothing. And if the companies building it can’t charge for it, that tells you something about how cheap it’s about to get for the companies using AI to replace you.

The uncomfortable truth is that most knowledge work was already hanging by a thread. We convinced ourselves that being good at email and meetings and slide decks was valuable because companies kept paying for it. But a lot of that work existed because of organizational dysfunction, not because it actually created value. Every time a coworker complained out loud that “this meeting could have been an email” that was, in reality, a warning shot.

AI doesn’t just replace workers. It exposes how much of the work didn’t need doing in the first place. That’s a harder thing to sit with than simple displacement, because it means the threat isn’t just that a machine can do your job, it’s that your job might not have been a real job to begin with. We’ve all just been collectively spinning the plates long enough that no one was really taking the time to look up and see which ones were close to falling.

Maybe this is catastrophic. Maybe I’m catastrophizing. Maybe the jobs don’t disappear, but instead transform, shift, evolve into something adjacent. That’s the way it’s always happened through every technological revolution is the line I keep hearing economists cite as reassurance. Maybe the person who was writing reports now manages the AI that writes reports, and everything works out.

But the speed is different this time. Previous waves of automation happened over decades. This one is happening over fiscal quarters. The gap between “AI can theoretically do your job” and “AI can do your job for the price of a sandwich” closed in about eighteen months, and there is no retraining program fast enough to keep up with a cost curve falling that fast.

The price of doing your job is collapsing.

The person who most likely pays for that is you.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: The Freakout Has Hit Mainstream
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: You’ve been trying to explain what’s coming for months. Then a stranger wrote it down and millions finally listened.
item.excerpt:encoded: You’ve been trying to explain what’s coming for months. Then a stranger wrote it down and millions finally listened.
item.content:encoded:
You’ve been trying to explain what’s coming for months. Then a stranger wrote it down and millions finally listened.

Image generated with AI.

You might have seen it in a text this week. Maybe an email. Maybe your brother sent it to the family group chat with nothing but “read this” underneath. No context or follow-up. Just a link to a post by a guy named Matt Shumer and the quiet desperation of someone who has been trying to say something for a very long time.

The article was called “Something Big Is Happening.” It racked up somewhere north of 70 million views on X (in this house we still call it twitter) in a couple of days. Shumer, who runs an AI company, laid out a case that AI is about to reshape the job market in ways most people haven’t started to think about. He cited Dario Amodei, the CEO of AI lab Anthropic, predicting that half of entry-level white-collar jobs could disappear within five years. He told people to get their financial houses in order. The tone was urgent. The claims were big.

And the internet did what it always does. It argued about who was right. Critics called it AI-generated word salad from a guy who sells AI tools and has every reason to make you nervous about the future. Fair enough. I’ve been guilty of a certain amount of catastrophizing recently, too. But whether Shumer is right or wrong misses the story.

The story is that a massive amount of people in tech finally had something they could forward to the normies in their life without sounding like a conspiracy theorist.

Tech industry people have been trying to explain what’s coming to their non-tech friends and family for months, maybe years, and failing. Dinner table conversations go nowhere. Texts that get one-word replies like, “Huh,” or “Interesting,” followed by a “You coming to the game this weekend or not?” As a tech person, you try to explain why this wave feels different and you sound like your uncle talking about Y2K. You’re the guy who spends too much time online. You’ve become the person at the party who won’t stop talking about the thing nobody is really paying attention to, while everyone else just wants to talk about fantasy football and online shopping.

Then Matt Shumer writes the essay that everyone finally feels like they can forward and not sound like they’ve fallen off the deep end. This one finally tells it how it is.

You send it to your brother-in-law who barely knows what ChatGPT even is. You send it to your dad. You send it to your college roommate with “Thoughts?” because what you really mean is “See? I wasn’t making it up.” You finally start to get the “holy sh*t!” feedback you’ve been waiting for.

Because when you’re the one saying it, you sound paranoid. But when a stranger says it and millions of people read it, suddenly it has weight. Suddenly it’s not just you being weird at Thanksgiving.

Megan McArdle of The Washington Post caught the real tension. She pointed out in a post on X (twitter, if you’re nasty) that if “AI-generated word salad” can convince millions of people that AI will reshape everything, maybe “you should update your priors in favor of AI being incredibly disruptive.” The medium proved the message, in its own uncomfortable way. But the people sharing the essay this week didn’t care about that paradox. They just needed something to point to that wasn’t them going off the rails again.

The loneliness of seeing something coming that the people around you don’t take seriously is something in the human condition that’s often felt, but seldom discussed. Parents trying to explain to their kids how to navigate social friendships because they were that age once, too, only to watch it fall on deaf ears. Those kids now a bit older and adults of their own trying to explain climate anxiety to their aging parents and failing.

Anyone who has ever seen something coming and tried to warn the people around them knows this feeling. You start to doubt yourself. Maybe they need to follow their own path because it’s not mine to take, you think. Maybe I’m in a bubble. Maybe I’m too online. Maybe it’s time to log off and touch grass.

Then someone else says it and suddenly everyone’s sharing it and you finally feel vindicated while also wanting to bang your head against the wall. It wasn’t the message, it was the messenger. Sometimes we have to find the right people to explain a situation for it to become real to those around us.

That’s what happened this week. Not the warning. The permission to finally take the warning seriously.

Whether Shumer’s timeline is right doesn’t change what happened. People who have been trying to talk about this and failing finally had something to send. If you were the one who sent it, you already know what I’m talking about.

And if you received it, the person who sent it to you wasn’t trying to scare you. They’ve been trying to reach you. They’ve been trying for a while.

And they’d really like it if they had someone to talk to about all of this.

Because if Shumer’s right, things are about to get wild.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: Everyone’s About to Find Out What They Actually Want to Do With Their Life
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: The answer was always there. It just couldn’t compete with rent.
item.excerpt:encoded: The answer was always there. It just couldn’t compete with rent.
item.content:encoded:
The answer was always there. It just couldn’t compete with rent.

Image generated with AI

ByteDance, the company that brought us TikTok, dropped an update to their video tool this week called Seedance 2.0 that turns two lines of text into something that looks like it came out of a Marvel trailer. Tom Cruise fighting Brad Pitt. Optimus Prime punching Godzilla on the Golden Gate Bridge. The cast of Friends reimagined as otters, one of them in a Rachel wig and everything. The general consensus?

Hollywood is cooked.

But the story underneath it all is so much more than a few mindblowing clips and an industry wondering if they’re on the brink of collapse. The story is that the tools to make basically anything are becoming free. Movie studios in your phone software. Music production from a prompt. Design, code, writing, animation, all of it collapsing toward zero cost faster than anyone can keep track of.

And when you combine that with something I touched on earlier this week, that the cost of running AI itself is falling by a factor of ten or more every year (and exponentially accelerating), you get a world where the barriers to making things just evaporate entirely.

So then what?

There’s a version of this story that’s terrifying, and I wrote that one, too. The jobs disappear, the math stops working in your favor, the sky falls in pieces. That’s real and it’s happening.

But there’s another version that I think is just as real, and very few people seem to be talking about it because optimism doesn’t get engagement.

When the tools are free and survival doesn’t require you to spend every waking hour doing something you’re mediocre at for someone else’s bottom line, people don’t just sit around.

They find their thing.

You probably already know what yours is. Or at least you have a suspicion. That thing you keep circling back to in quiet moments, the project you’ve been meaning to start, the skill you’ve been wanting to learn, the idea you never chased because the economics didn’t make sense.

Maybe it’s writing. Maybe it’s woodworking. Maybe it’s some niche corner of engineering that no one would pay for but you find genuinely beautiful. Maybe it’s something you don’t even have a word for yet, because you’ve never had the opportunity to slow down even for a second in the nonstop hustle of life to listen to what the universe was trying to tell you.

For most of human history, the answer to “what are you here for” has been “whatever keeps me fed.” You trade your hours for survival and if you’re lucky you get evenings and weekends to dabble in the thing you actually care about. Most people took that deal because what other choice did they have? The tools were expensive, the barriers were high, and the economy needed interchangeable parts.

Every single part of that equation is breaking.

UVA tracked humanities majors dropping from 49% to 38% in a decade while STEM climbed to 44%. Everyone chased the code. Did the responsible thing. And now the machines are learning that code better, faster, and for essentially nothing. A generation of intense focus on STEM may have ironically hastened the pace to its own obsolescence.

But the point isn’t that STEM kids lose and humanities kids win. That framing is too small. The point is that the whole concept of “study what pays” is dissolving, and what’s replacing it is something way more personal and way harder to put on a career aptitude test.

What do you actually want to make?

David Brooks wrote a few years ago about AI missing what he called a humanistic core. Passion, pain, longings. The stuff no training data set is going to truly ever capture. He’s right, but I think he’s underselling it. Because it’s not just that humans in general have something AI doesn’t. It’s that every individual human has something no other individual human has either. Some unique, particular, deeply personal version of “this is what I bring to the world.” We’ve spent the last century burying that under the demands of an economy that needed everybody to be essentially the same. Cogs in a wheel. Well, the economy is about to stop needing that.

The things we used to call hobbies and side projects and “what I’d do if I won the lottery” are quietly becoming the actual economy. The mechanic who films car restorations because he loves it. The accountant who finally writes her book. The nurse who starts a community garden that becomes a real business. The software engineer who makes pottery because she wants to, not because a life coach told her to pivot.

None of these people are asking permission. The tools are already here. What’s catching up is the realization that this isn’t a niche thing happening on the margins. It’s the whole picture of what our future looks like.

Some people will find their thing through art. Some through building with their hands. Some through faith or community. Some will go deeper into STEM, not because a job listing demands it but because they love the elegance of the math.

That shift from obligation to choice might be the most underrated story of the entire AI era. The specifics are different for every person. That’s the whole point. It’s uncategorizable. It’s personal. It’s eight billion people’s individual versions of purpose, and no algorithm is going to sort it into a pie chart.

Maybe I’m being naive and the disruption hits too fast and too hard for something this optimistic to survive contact with reality. I’ve written about the hard version of this story twice this week already, and I meant every word of it. But I keep watching people who’ve lost their old reasons for doing things stumble into new ones that are better, and it’s happening quietly and constantly and in ways no one is really tracking because it doesn’t fit the narrative of collapse.

The dust is going to settle, eventually. It always does. And what I think is growing underneath all of this, faster than most people realize, is an enormous number of people who’ve been waiting their entire lives to make something that matters to them, finally getting the chance.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: What Happens When Someone Deepfakes Your Kid?
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: New AI technology may seem cool, but it comes at a price.
item.excerpt:encoded: New AI technology may seem cool, but it comes at a price.
item.content:encoded:
New AI technology may seem cool, but it comes at a price.

Image generated with AI

You might remember a scam that was going around a few years ago. Someone calls a grandparent, says they have their grandkid, and they’re right outside the school. They know the kid’s name, their teacher’s name, what grade they’re in, and the layout of the school grounds. Wire money now or something terrible happens.

Except, they were never anywhere near the school. They were half a world away with a phone and whatever you posted on Facebook last Tuesday.

And it worked. It worked a lot. Not because the people who fell for it were stupid, but because the scam exploits something genuinely decent in people. They’d rather lose money than gamble on the off chance that it’s real and their loved one is actually in danger. That’s not stupidity. It’s love. And scammers have been feeding on it for years.

We’ll call that “the phone call era.” Scary, sure, but kind of crude. You could hang up, call the school, verify. The panic was real but the tools were primitive.

Then voice cloning showed up. You only need about three seconds of someone’s audio to clone their voice with something like 90% accuracy. Your kid posted a TikTok last week? That’s more than enough. Now the person calling grandma doesn’t just claim to have the kid. They put the kid on the phone. Crying and begging and sounding exactly like them, because in every way that matters to a panicking grandparent, it is them.

The defense we collectively came up with was a code word. Security experts, the FBI, everybody said the same thing: establish a family code word, something only you would know, and if someone calls claiming to be your kid or another loved one, ask for it. Kind of low-tech and old-fashioned, like a speakeasy password for the AI age. But it worked.

Then the FBI put out a warning last December about criminals altering social media photos into fake kidnapping proof-of-life shots. Your kid’s face, bound and terrified in some basement. Their advice was to zoom in and look for errors. Missing tattoos, weird proportions, the usual AI tells.

That warning came out just in December. But in just two months, it’s already obsolete.

You see where this is going, right?

This week, ByteDance, the same company that created TikTok, released an updated version of their AI video creation tool, Seedance 2.0. All it needs is a text prompt and a photo and it can generate a realistic real-world looking video in minutes. Output that looks like it came out of a movie studio.

Within hours of its release, people were generating video of things like Brad Pitt and Tom Cruise fistfighting on a rooftop. Full video. Facial expressions, dialogue, the works. SAG-AFTRA condemned it. Disney sent a cease and desist. Hollywood is furious about what this means for actors and intellectual property, and yeah, that conversation matters.

But that’s not the conversation we’re about to have here.

Because a tool that can generate photorealistic video of any person from a handful of photos lives under the same corporate roof as the platform that your kid has been posting videos of themselves performing sweet dance moves for the past several years. We collectively helped them develop this tech by uploading our videos day after day after day.

So now, run the scam again. Someone calls grandma. The voice on the phone sounds exactly like her grandchild. And then video arrives. Her grandchild is in a room she doesn’t recognize, scared, crying, looking directly into the camera and saying her name. Every detail is perfect because the source material was already public.

What’s the security defense now?

Zooming in doesn’t work when there aren’t any errors to find. The secret code word goes out the window when you think you’re watching them be tortured on a live stream. Every safeguard you set in place for the previous version of this scam just got leapfrogged.

This all happened in less than three years. From phone calls with a name and a location to cloned voices to doctored photos to generated video.

So, now what are we supposed to do to protect ourselves?

Set up a code word because it may still prove valuable.

Tell your kids to be careful what they post, knowing they probably won’t be because they’re kids, but you do it anyway.

Maybe you train yourself to hang up and call back on a number you trust before you act, but honestly I don’t know how many people could actually pull that off at 2 AM with their kid’s face on a screen, screaming for help.

The conversation about deepfakes keeps centering on celebrities and politicians, people with lawyers and publicists and entire teams managing their digital likeness.

But the twelve-year old posting dance videos on TikTok doesn’t have a publicist.

The grandparent who picks up the phone in the middle of the night doesn’t have a forensic analysis team.

All they have is love and fear and a three-second window to make a decision, and, hopefully, a special code word just between them that they never in a million years thought they’d have to use.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: The Jobs Aren’t Coming Back. They’re Not Even Staying There.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: The outsourced jobs aren’t coming back to America. But they’re not staying in India either. This week, India’s own leaders said so out loud.
item.excerpt:encoded: The outsourced jobs aren’t coming back to America. But they’re not staying in India either. This week, India’s own leaders said so out loud.
item.content:encoded:
The outsourced jobs aren’t coming back to America. But they’re not staying in India either. This week, India’s own leaders said so out loud.

Image generated with AI

There’s a version of this story that will sound very familiar to Americans.

Because a couple of decades ago, your customer service calls started getting routed to Manila.

Your tax prep got done in Bangalore, while you were sleeping.

The town factory closed and the work went overseas because it was cheaper for the businesses, and you were told that would make things back home cheaper for you, too.

You were told this was Economics 101, that it was inevitable, and that you’d adapt.

You were told we really didn’t want those jobs, anyway.

Except, the only problem is some people didn’t adapt. Heck, some whole towns and cities didn’t adapt. Things went a little sideways, and economic anger and distress took hold, and collectively instead of blaming the business owners who made the decision to move those jobs, or the politicians who let it happen, we started blaming the person who took those jobs directly. We found an “other” to blame.

Yeah, a lot didn’t go as it was pitched to us.

But at least you could point to where the jobs went. They went somewhere, it just wasn’t in your backyard anymore. And if we could point to a place where they went, we could find a way to get those jobs to come back from that place. The “others” who had built a life half a world away would have to figure it out, just like we did.

The problem is, no one is asking what happens when those jobs that went overseas get outsourced into oblivion.

This week, some of the people on the other end of those jobs held a summit. India’s biggest AI event ever with a quarter million attendees packed into a convention center in Delhi. And what they heard from their own industry leaders is the real story Americans need to hear.

Vinod Khosla, a Silicon Valley VC born in India, told a room of participants that IT services and outsourcing operations will “almost completely disappear” within five years. A billionaire VC just casually talking about possibly the biggest disruption of our lifetimes like he was reading the weather.

Then Vineet Nayyar, the former CEO of HCL, one of the biggest IT employers in the country, stood in front of a room full of people whose livelihoods depend on those jobs and said Indian IT companies will focus on turning profits, not on being job creators.

This wasn’t in a leaked memo or an investor call. This was said at a public summit, to an audience full of people it’s about. If you listen closely, they’re no longer sending warning signals about a potential future. They’re telling you it’s already been decided.

Meanwhile, the summit itself could barely keep its internet running. Mobile data was patchy. Digital payments were down. Attendees were posting about hour-long lines and no water. A quarter million people gathered to talk about the future of artificial intelligence, and the venue couldn’t hold a cell signal.

It kind of makes you want to laugh and ask: “Is the technological utopian future in the room with us right now?”

Mock if you will, but a little congested internet traffic is not going to make the exponential growth of AI slow down anytime soon. (They have better wifi, anyway.)

Imagine you work in IT in Bangalore. You’ve spent fifteen years building a career that exists because American companies wanted cheaper labor. You go to this summit because it’s the biggest thing your industry has ever put together, and while you’re standing in line waiting to get in, the guy on stage is calmly explaining that your job will be gone before your kid finishes elementary school. And you can’t even google what he said because the wifi doesn’t work.

The workers of one of the countries that received the largest portions of American outsourced jobs are being told by the people who run those companies that the work is finished.

The $1.1 billion the Indian government committed this week to AI startups? That money is for replacing those workers, all in the name of staying up with the competition of other countries. An international “keeping up with the Joneses,” if you will.

So, let’s recap:

The jobs were in America. They went to India (and other countries). Americans wanted the jobs back. India’s industry leaders instead told the world that those jobs are going on a server rack at some point between now and sixty months from now. There’s no longer going to be a call center to be mad at. You won’t even be able to point to it on a map.

An AI company CEO posted recently that his own tools had made him redundant at his actual job. This is no longer “let’s move the entry and mid-level jobs overseas.” This is “AI is going to be doing all of the jobs, on a worldwide level, restless public be damned.”

Dario Amodei, who runs the AI lab Anthropic, has said publicly that AI will eliminate half of entry-level white-collar jobs within five years. Khosla is saying the same about IT jobs. These are the people building our replacements, and they keep volunteering the timeline to anyone who will listen. The last major economic employment shift happened behind closed doors. Jack Welch-era boardrooms and private equity firms like Bain Capital decided where the jobs went, and by the time anyone outside those rooms found out, it was already done. This time? They’re doing it right in front of us and sending warning shots anytime they get the chance.

My daughter graduates from high school in a little over two years, and I can tell by the majority of conversations being had, most people are just moving along as if it’s business as usual.

Parents talking about planning for their kids to be doctors and lawyers. Students going to career fairs that look an awful lot like the ones I attended at the turn of the last century.

The world probably won’t look drastically different by the time she walks across that stage in mid-2028. But by the time she’s done with college in 2034? I don’t think the career most of these kids are planning for will exist in the shape they’re imagining it.

Maybe predicting disruption is just what VCs and AI company CEOs do at conferences and interviews, and none of this is different from every other cycle of “technology-will-change-everything,” but I’ve been watching the way these particular people say these particular things, and it doesn’t sound like a sales pitch.

They’re not nervous about it.

They talk like someone who has internalized, debated, and are now embracing our inevitable future.

They’re standing on stages saying it with their names attached, to rooms full of people who will be directly affected, and the rooms are just sitting there hootin’ and hollerin’ and having a grand ol’ time.

The problem is we collectively don’t even seem to be having these conversations at all. We’re just going through the motions, living our lives.

From my perspective, most people seem to be sitting in a worldwide pot, oblivious to how hot it’s getting, while the water’s now almost to a boil.

And their biggest concern is when will the wifi start working.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: You Don’t Need Permission to Build Anymore
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: The one-person company is no longer a fantasy. It’s real, and it’s fabulous.
item.excerpt:encoded: The one-person company is no longer a fantasy. It’s real, and it’s fabulous.
item.content:encoded:
The one-person company is no longer a fantasy. It’s real, and it’s fabulous.

Image generated with AI

Aaron Sneed has fifteen employees and none of them are people.

That sounds like the opening to a dystopian piece, and a year ago I might have written it that way. We’ve been doing a lot of catastrophizing lately. The sky is falling, the jobs are leaving, the robots are stealing your lunch and stuffing you in lockers (ok, I didn’t write that last one yet but now I think maybe I should). But something else is happening at the same time, and it’s worth sitting with for a minute.

Sneed is 40, lives in Florida, and runs a defense-tech company. By himself. He built what he calls “The Council,” which is fifteen custom AI agents handling everything from legal review to supply chain logistics to HR. He trained his AI agents to argue with him. On purpose. “I don’t want a bunch of yes agents,” he told Business Insider. “I trained them purposefully to give me pushback.”

When he needs a decision, he puts the question to all fifteen simultaneously. They cross-check each other. They flag when one of them is making things up. It’s less “boss and assistant” and more “one guy running a very opinionated boardroom.”

Twenty years ago, that setup would have required a floor of an office building and a payroll that kept an accountant busy full-time.

Meanwhile, up in Vancouver, Jay Moreno was trying to do things the old way. He’d spent twenty years building tech companies across Latin America, managing rooms full of engineers, running the kind of operations that take months just to staff. So when he had an idea for a new software product, he’d always run the same play: find a partner, hire a small team, raise some money, spend at least a year building.

Only this time when inspiration struck, he started tinkering with AI coding tools instead. Just to test the idea. He didn’t dream of a possibility where he could build the whole thing himself. And then the testing became the building, and the building emerged as a finished product in four months. Just one person, alone, with an AI writing assistant handling the code while he handled decisions.

By month four, he’d shipped a fully working platform: automated intake conversations, customer tracking, billing through Stripe, a marketable website, the works. “A well-funded startup with a full engineering team would have taken 12 to 18 months,” he wrote.

The tried and true plan didn’t fail. It became irrelevant.

Now, Moreno isn’t someone who just watched a how-to YouTube tutorial and got to work. He managed engineering teams for twenty years. He knows what breaks at scale, which corners you can’t cut, why certain design choices will haunt you two years later. “AI gave me speed,” he wrote. “My experience gave the AI direction.”

That’s the piece people miss when they hear most of these stories. The sky is falling outlook is about AI replacing expertise. But viewed through a slightly more rose-colored lens and it can actually be closer to the opposite.

The barrier to starting something used to be: raise capital, headcount, office space, and six months of hiring people who might not even work well together. Now the barrier is taste, judgment, and agency. The people who’ve spent years learning how things actually work suddenly don’t need anyone’s permission to go build.

I don’t want to sand the edges off this. The same forces making one-person companies possible are also making a lot of people’s jobs disappear. I’m not going to pretend the math works out cleanly for everyone.

But think about who this unlocks. The mid-career engineer who always had a product idea but couldn’t afford to quit and spend a year assembling a team. The consultant sitting on enough industry knowledge to build something real, if only “building something real” didn’t used to require a dozen people and plenty of runway. That wall just got very thin.

These are just two examples, but there are thousands of these stories beginning to emerge. Sneed and Moreno are just on the bleeding edge of something people are only now beginning to realize is even possible.

Moreno said it himself: “If you told me two years ago that a single founder could ship this scope in this timeline, I would not have believed you.”

He’s not alone in that disbelief. Most of us are still operating on old assumptions about what it takes to build something. How many people you need. How much money you need to raise or save. How much time you have before that money runs dry. Under the old-world model, most people would give up before they even started. It just wasn’t realistic.

None of that has to be true anymore. There’s eight billion people out there each with their own unique strengths, beliefs, and (dare I say) dreams.

The only thing left for them to do is take the leap and start building.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.

item.title: Your Dry Cleaner Isn’t Ready for AI. That’s an Opportunity.
item.dc:creator: Greg Dessau
item.description: Every major tech shift creates a class of people who grab the new tool and sell it. The 1990s web designer knew this. You might be next.
item.excerpt:encoded: Every major tech shift creates a class of people who grab the new tool and sell it. The 1990s web designer knew this. You might be next.
item.content:encoded:
Every major tech shift creates a class of people who grab the new tool and sell it. The 1990s web designer knew this. You might be next.

Image generated with AI

Janet Feenstra bakes bread now. A year ago she was editing academic papers in Malmö. She saw AI coming and made a call: get out before it was too late. “I have a lot more fun,” she told The Guardian, and then added, “but I don’t want to be grateful to AI for this. I’m still a little bit bitter.”

A thousand miles west, Jacqueline Bowman was doing content marketing in California until clients started telling her how great it was “that we don’t need writers any more” while handing her AI-generated articles to fact-check and repair. Work that used to take two hours now took four, at half the pay, because sixty percent of what the AI produced was wrong. She’s studying to be a therapist now.

Hard not to feel for these women. As someone who spent nearly five years writing television and radio ad copy, I know all too well how quickly the copywriting well dried up.

The Guardian ran both their stories under the headline “The big AI job swap.” It’s sympathetic, well-reported, and it gets something real: the fear is rational, and both women made entirely understandable choices. They saw a wave coming and stepped off the beach.

But the article is missing a character.

In 1996, nobody woke up at eighteen and decided to become a web designer, because the job didn’t exist yet. Then every pizza shop and dentist’s office in America realized they needed a website and had absolutely no idea how to build one. So a whole class of people, often self-taught, often coming from unrelated fields, learned HTML and started knocking on doors. Five thousand dollars for a brochure site with a photo of your storefront and a phone number that wasn’t even clickable. The business owner didn’t need to understand the technology. They just needed someone who did. That gold rush lasted the better part of two decades before sites like Squarespace and Wix made it easy enough that your nephew could do it over Thanksgiving.

That’s exactly what’s happening right now with AI. Every business “needs AI” in the same vague, panicky way every business “needed a website” in 1997. They’ve read the headlines. They’re scared. And they don’t know what a chatbot actually is or what it would do for them specifically. Census Bureau data shows business formations climbed 4.5% in January alone. People are already making this move, and many others are sure to follow.

Ruchir Baronia quit his job at Meta to start a company called Frontdesk that helps businesses automate phone conversations with AI. The dental office fielding forty calls a day about hours and insurance. The contractor who misses client calls while he’s twenty feet up a ladder. Baronia builds the thing that answers those phones. He told Business Insider that “code written alone in my bedroom could reach people I would never meet.” He’s the web designer repackaged for 2026, except the knowledge gap he’s selling is growing into an even bigger behemoth than web 2.0, with growth increasing from eight billion to twelve billion in a single year.

Now, Baronia’s the success story, but we shouldn’t sugarcoat this and treat it as a gold rush for everyone. Because when Nadia Privalikhina built her AI automation agency and wrote about it on LinkedIn, she expressed the kind of honesty you don’t often see from someone actively selling a service. Half her potential clients had budgets under two thousand dollars. The generic “I’ll slap a chatbot on your website” pitch is already oversaturated. Ninety-five percent of enterprise AI pilots fail because companies want documented results, not demos. “Connecting blocks in no-code tools is easy,” she wrote. “The hard part is figuring out which blocks to connect for this specific business.” The person who can install the tool is getting commoditized. The person who understands your actual Tuesday afternoon and where the friction lives is still rare and still worth paying for.

The dry cleaner two blocks from my house still writes phone numbers on little paper tags. The auto shop on the corner has a voicemail system from 2004 that cuts you off mid-sentence. Neither of those businesses are unaware that AI exists. They hear about it constantly, mostly in coverage that swings between wonder and catastrophe and never lands on anything they can actually use. What they don’t have is a person who understands their specific problem and can translate a technology into something that fixes it.

That gap between the tool and the person who needs it is a job. It’s always been a job. The web designers had it. The electricians who wired the first factories had it. And right now, today, it exists again, and it’s sitting there waiting for people who might otherwise be reading Guardian articles about how to escape the permanent underclass.

Bowman and Feenstra weren’t wrong. Their math was correct and their pivots are legitimate. Not everyone has the temperament to cold-call their dentist and offer to automate the front desk. Some people genuinely want out of the digital economy, and baking bread with your hands is a serious answer to the question the economy is asking.

But somewhere between fleeing the tool and building the next billion-dollar startup, there’s a much quieter move that doesn’t make headlines. Someone right now is learning what the dry cleaner needs and figuring out how to provide it. No pitch deck. No venture round. Just a person who understands the tool and a person who needs it.

That job never has a name until five years after everyone’s already doing it.

Greg Dessau writes about what it feels like to be a person while machines eat everything. More at Human Offset. Get it in your inbox at humanoffset.substack.com.
